gold_text: * * *  HISTORICAL DOCUMENT CONVERTED FROM CERNER  * * *        03/14/2006      Russell Royal, MD  843 Dweebie Rd, Iuka IL       RE: Pick, Toby Sleesman  MEDICAL RECORD #: [000000]  DATE OF BIRTH:  12/09/1992    Dear Doctor Kirchner:    I had the pleasure of seeing Seymour Luty in our Long-Term Survivor Clinic for  an annual visit.    As you may recall, Terrance is a 13-year-old female originally diagnosed with  B-progenitor acute lymphoblastic leukemia in 12/20/1995.  She received a  standard three-drug induction.  She was found to have CNS-II disease following  induction chemotherapy.  At the end of her four-week induction chemotherapy, she  was found to be in remission and then was treated with chemotherapy per the BFM  protocol for standard risk acute lymphoblastic leukemia.  Terrance did well with  her therapy and completed all scheduled chemotherapy in 09/20/1998.  She was  found to be in complete remission at that time.    As far as her interval history, she is accompanied by her mother and great aunt  today.  They state that she has been doing well.  They have no significant  concerns.  She has had no intercurrent illnesses, no hospitalizations.  She  complains of no nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.  She states that she  has no discomfort or pain, no burning pain.  No headaches.  She has no shortness of  breath or chest pain.  She has no changes in her vision or mental status.  She  continues to wear glasses.    As far as her social history, Terrance continues to live at home with her mother,  her stepfather, four cats, and one dog.  She has a 21-year-old sister who no longer  lives in the home.  Her mother continues to sell Bunkie  and also works at an  appraisal office.  Her stepfather works outside the home.  Terrance is now in the  8th grade.  She continues to be a very good student and continues to be on the  honor roll and receives straight A's.   She is active in softball and has many  friends.  She states that she did begin her menstrual cycle in 04/20/2005 and  is regular, occuring once a month.  She does have cramps, but she does take Tylenol   for this which does help.    MEDICATIONS:  None.    ALLERGIES:  No known drug allergies.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  VITAL SIGNS:  Temperature is 36.6 degrees Celsius.  Pulse 66, blood pressure is  113/51, respirations 18.  Height is 156.5 cm, weight is 83.5 kg.  GENERAL:  Terrance is an obese young adolescent in no acute distress.  HEENT:  Normocephalic.  She has a well-distributed regrowth of hair to entire  scalp.  Pupils equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation.  EOMs are intact.  Funduscopic exam is benign.  Sclerae are nonicteric.  Nares are patent.  No  discharge.  Oropharynx and posterior pharynx:  No ulcerative lesions or mucositis  noted.  Tympanic membranes are pearly gray with good light reflects.  NECK:  Supple.  LYMPHATICS:  There is no lymphadenopathy noted to any lymph node stations.  CHEST:  Clear to auscultation throughout lung fields.  Respirations even and  unlabored.  CARDIOVASCULAR:  Regular rate and rhythm.  No audible murmurs.  ABDOMEN:  Soft, rounded.  No hepatosplenomegaly or palpable masses noted.  GENITALIA:  She is a normal adolescent female with breast development, hair under  her axillary, and has begun her menstrual cycle.  SKIN:  Clear and free of rashes.  NEUROLOGIC:  Grossly intact.    Laboratory evaluation reveals a white blood cell count of 7, hemoglobin of 13.6,  hematocrit of 38.2.  Neutrophils 54.6, lymphocytes 30.6, monocytes 11.9.  ANC 3840.  Creatinine 0.59.  Bilirubin is 0.6.  AST is 21, ALT is 12.  Urinalysis is within  normal limits.    ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:  Terrance is a 13-year-old female with a history of acute  lymphoblastic leukemia.  She is now eight years from completion of therapy.    She continues to be without evidence of recurrent disease by  physical examination   and laboratory evaluation.  I spoke with Terrance and her mother regarding   some of the concerns regarding late effects from her treatment.  She did receive  triple intrathecals as well as methotrexate. I explained to Terrance that we have seen some learning difficulties and difficulties in memory; however, Terrance is obviously doing   very well in school and does not have any effects from this.  She also was treated with 75 mg/sq m of Adriamycin, which is a low-dose Adriamycin, but she could possibly have some cardiomyopathy  from this.  Therefore, we did check with an echocardiogram today with the  fractional shortening and ejection fraction within normal limits.  I also explained  to Terrance's mother that even though Adriamycin was at a relatively low dose, we  will continue to monitor her at least every three to five years with  echocardiograms.  She also received cyclophosphamide 3 g/sq m, and we would like to  continue to follow her for possible hemorrhage cystitis or renal tubular problems.  We did obtain a urinalysis which was within normal limits, and certain chemistries  which were within normal limits also.  She also is at risk for secondary  malignancies due to her cyclophosphamide dose.  Therefore, a CBCD was obtained  which was normal.  She is also at possible risk for early menopause which I  discussed with West Palm Beach  and her mother.  She did start her menses in 04/20/2005,  and she appears to be having a regular menstrual cycle every month.  She also did receive   a fair amount of steroids which can lead to osteopenia as well as increased fracture risk.  Her  mother states that she feels that Terrance continues a good intake of calcium in  her diet and does do weightbearing activities.  Terrance and her mother were in  agreement of our management plan, and I would like to continue to see Terrance in  our clinic once a year.        Sincerely,          DOC #:  53973      DICT  JOB #:  [000000]    D: 03/14/2006  T: 03/16/2006  I: 03/16/2006  (Electronically signed on 03/22/2006 at 16:24)  __________________________________________________  Molloy      (Electronically signed on 05/28/2006    at 12:01)  __________________________________________________  Franzen      D: 03/14/2006

09/03/2022    Name: Puno: 12/09/1992  Date of Service: 09/03/2022     Main Hospital Outreach Locations     Owingsville    Oran Wolinsky, MD  Clyde Belasco, MD  Shiraz Maskatia, MD  Guillermo Guirguis, MD Shaylyn Paganelli, MD  Yides Dooley, MD  Burton Crouch, MD  Naquan Sinno, MD Tiffany Mcpherson, MD  Guillermina Niazi, MD  Lavern Levin, MD  Peyton Bisson, MD Lovely Akay, MD  Azriel Drummond, MD  Baby Skaug, BSN, RN  Hunter Akl, RN-NIC     .FETAL CARDIOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE     Garcia, MD  1685A Commercial Way  Perinatal Diagnostic Center Santa Cruz  Santa Cruz,  CA 95065      Dear Dr. Kaya and Ms. Fletes,    I had the pleasure of seeing your patient, Monroe Gretchen Voigt, in our fetal cardiology clinic in Oceana  on 09/03/2022 for a new consultation. Indications for the fetal echocardiogram included: family history of congenital heart disease. She has a personal hx of atrial septal defect (ASD).    History:  Monroe is a 29-year old G2P1 patient with a single intrauterine pregnancy with an EDD 01/18/2023 currently at 20+6 weeks. No complications have been noted during this pregnancy. At the time of this visit, there were no identified fetal anomalies. Monroe is taking zofran and sumatriptan during this pregnancy. There is no known significant family history of congenital heart disease or cardiac related genetic disorders.      Fetal Echocardiogram:  A complete 2D fetal echocardiogram with color and spectral Doppler was obtained to evaluate cardiac anatomy, function, and hemodynamics. I personally viewed the echo images. The fetal heart rate was within normal range, and the fetus measured appropriately for dates. The fetal echocardiogram findings were reassuring. No structural cardiac defects were identified. Normal cardiac position and connections were seen. All four  cardiac valves were functioning normally. The right and left ventricles were of normal size and had qualitatively normal systolic function. There was no pericardial effusion or other evidence of hydrops. Note an asd is a normal part of fetal life so it cannot be ruled out prenatally.    Counseling and Recommendations:  I reviewed the findings of today's fetal echocardiogram with Monroe, which are reassuring. I recommend a routine postnatal follow up at 1 month of age with me in Bronx  office given Monroe's hx of ASD, that ASDs have a higher rate of inheritance and that they cannot be ruled out prenatally.    I discussed the limitations of fetal echocardiography, including but not limited to the inability to rule out atrial or ventricular septal defects, structural valve abnormalities, total or partial anomalous pulmonary venous return, coronary anomalies, patent ductus arteriosus, and coarctation of the aorta.    At this time, no follow-up is recommended in fetal cardiology clinic unless new concerns arise. At the time of this visit, Silver Creek  was planning to deliver at unknown. Based on the findings of today's fetal echocardiogram, I recommend no changes to the delivery planning. She will follow up with her primary care physicians as scheduled. I spent a total of 30 minutes of face-to-face time with the patient, over half of which involved counseling/discussion.     Thank you for allowing me to participate in Rowlett 's care. If you have any questions or concerns, please do not to hesitate to contact me. I can be reached at my office number at 999-999-9999.      Sincerely,  Mohindra, MD  Pediatric and Fetal Cardiology  Stanford Children's Health    For information on our fetal cardiology program and locations, please visit our website: www.example.com          Greenspan, MD

gold_text: 9999-9999 SPECIMEN SUBMITTED: 1. LUMBAR TUMOR, (FSA AND TP); 2. LUMBAR TUMOR; 3. FILUM TERMINALE; 4. LUMBAR ARACHOID TISSUE; 5. NEUROMA TUMOR TISSUE LABORATORY OF NEUROPATHOLOGY Ronnie Runyan, M.D. - Director ROOM R-241, TELEPHONE (999-999-9999, FAX (999-999-9999 OPERATION:  Lumbar laminectomy for tumor resection, L5. CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS:  Lesion L5 lumbar. FROZEN SECTION DIAGNOSIS: FSA/TPA:  SPINAL CORD, L5 MASS, BIOPSY                --   EPENDYMOMA, GRADE II                          (Gratzinger/Horoupian) GROSS DESCRIPTION:  Five specimens are received each labeled with the patient's name "Villalobos, Richie" and the medical record number. Specimen one is received fresh from the OR (subsequently placed in formalin), additionally labeled "lumbar tumor", and consists of a 0.4 cm in greatest dimension tan-white soft tissue fragment. A touch prep is performed labeled TPA. A portion of the specimen is then submitted for frozen section diagnosis labeled FSA. The specimen is not exhausted. The tissue not submitted for frozen section is submitted for permanents as A1. Specimen two is received fresh (subsequently placed in formalin), additionally labeled "lumbar tumor", and consists of a 1.5 x 1.5 x 1.5 cm tan-red soft tissue nodule admixed with a 0.8 x 0.8 x 0.3 cm aggregate of soft tan-gray tissue. Two-thirds of the specimen is banked and donated to research (in the fresh state). The remaining tissue is further sectioned and submitted in its entirety for permanents as B1. Specimen three is received in formalin, additionally labeled "filum terminale", and consists of a 1 x 0.1 x 0.1 cm, stringy, red-tan soft tissue fragment which is submitted in toto as C1. Specimen four is received in formalin, additionally labeled "lumbar arachnoid tissue", and consists of a 1.3 x 0.4 x 0.2 cm gray-pink soft tissue fragment which is submitted in toto as D1. Specimen five is received in formalin, additionally labeled "neuroma tumor tissue", and consists of a 1.8 x 0.8 x 0.3 cm aggregate of tan-red, focally circumscribed soft tissue which is submitted in toto as E1.      Fatemi for NEUROPATH/cb MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:  Permanent sections confirm the frozen section diagnosis of ependymoma (FSA).  Sections of the lumbar tumor specimen show a dense proliferation of monomorphic neoplastic cells in papillary arrangements.  The neoplastic display monomorphic rounded nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.  Scattered perivascular pseudo-rosettes are identified.  Mitoses are rare to absent.  Immunohistochemical staining shows that the neoplastic cells stain positive for GFAP and fail to stain for transthyretin. EMA stains neoplastic cells and highlights occasional ependymal lumina. DIAGNOSIS: LUMBAR, TUMOR, BIOPSY (FSA, A)      --   EPENDYMOMA, PAPILLARY VARIANT, WHO GRADE II LUMBAR, TUMOR, RESECTION (B)      --   EPENDYMOMA, PAPILLARY VARIANT, WHO GRADE II FILUM TERMINALE, BIOPSY  (C)      --   NO SIGNIFICANT NEUROPATHOLOGIC ABNORMALITY LUMBAR, ARACHNOID TISSUE, BIOPSY (D)      --   NO SIGNIFICANT NEUROPATHOLOGIC ABNORMALITY NEUROMA, RESECTION (E)      --   EPENDYMOMA, PAPILLARY VARIANT, WHO GRADE II Mccray/BURTELOW/D'APUZZO/Luster/Slabaugh cb/10/12/2004 I have personally reviewed the specimen and agree with the interpretation above. Everlean Behringer M.D. Electronically signed 10/22/2004

question: Provide a detailed obstetrical history for this patient.
evidence: She is G-III, P-I, 0, 2, 1. She has a son. Her age at first term pregnancy is 35.
gold_text: COMPREHENSIVE BREAST ONCOLOGY TUMOR BOARD    IDENTIFICATION: Mrs. Teixeira is a 48-year-old premenopausal female with  newly diagnosed DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ) of the right breast and a  stable lung nodule.    HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS: Mrs. Teixeira has been followed by yearly  mammograms. On a recent mammogram done on 05/18/2003, an abnormality  was found. A development of a 1 cm area of heterogeneous  microcalcifications located in the right breast at the 7 o'clock position  in the lower outer quadrant located 9 cm from the nipple. The patient was  advised to have that area biopsied as it was suspicious for malignancy. Of  note, there are no suspicious lesions in her left breast. On 05/25/2003, the patient underwent core needle biopsy of the suspicious area in  her right breast. The pathology report showed diffuse patches of  high-grade DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ), that measured 2 mm in greatest  dimension, solid and comedo type, with microcalcifications, located at the  7 o'clock position in the right breast, 9 cm from the nipple. No ER/PR  staining was done at that time.    The patient has a lung nodule measuring approximately 1.7 x 1.5 cm in the  superior segment of the right lower lobe of her lung. It was discovered in  1999. The patient reports that it was biopsied in 09/21/1999 and  although we do not have a report of the biopsy the patient tells us that it  was diagnosed as a benign hamartoma. We do have chest CT scan reports  dating from 1999 yearly to a recent one done on 06/08/2003, all of which  describe this nodule as remaining stable. It has not grown or changed in  approximately four years. Today, Mrs. Tesoriero presents to Breast Tumor  Board in order to discuss her therapy options and to obtain  recommendations.    BREAST CANCER RISK FACTORS: The patient is premenopausal, menarche at age  13. She is G-III, P-I, 0, 2, 1. She has a son. Her age at first term  pregnancy is 35.  She breast fed her son. The patient is having regular  periods, last menstrual period was 06/01/2003. Prior biopsies 1.    ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE USE: For one year in 1976.    SMOKING: From 1975 to 2000 the patient smoked about one cigarette per day.  She no longer smokes.    ALCOHOL: Two drinks per week, mostly wine.    ENVIRONMENTAL EXPOSURES: None.    RADIATION EXPOSURE: None.    FAMILY HISTORY: Maternal grandmother with breast cancer diagnosed when she  was 75 years old. She had a mastectomy. She had cancer in only one  breast. Also, she had lung cancer. She passed away of lung cancer  complications. Paternal grandmother with liver cirrhosis. Her mother is  age 68, alive and healthy. Her father is age 77, alive and has myasthenia  gravis. The patient has two sisters, one age 46 and one age 47. One has  benign uterine growth which the patient cannot describe other than tumors  which sometimes need to be removed.    PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  1. Hypertension.  2. Chronic UTI (urinary tract infection) starting at age 9, status post  V-plasty.  3. Status post car accident with multiple surgeries now with chronic   neck and back problems.  4. History of hepatitis A and mononucleosis.  5. History of basal cell carcinomas which have been excised.    ALLERGIES: Penicillin, anaphylactic shock when takes penicillin.    MEDICATIONS: Avapro and lisinopril.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: On review of systems the patient reports having  increased fatigue and stress since she found out this diagnosis, although  she reports no weight loss and no other symptoms. Specifically, no  problems with her eyes, no problems with her ears, nose or throat, no heart  problems, no pulmonary problems including shortness of breath, chest pain,  cough or trouble breathing, no gastrointestinal problems specifically no  abdominal pain, no diarrhea, no constipation, no nausea and vomiting, no  genitourinary problems, no hematological problems, no neurological  problems  specifically no headaches, no dizziness, no loss of consciousness. The  patient reports no endocrine problems and no psychiatric problems. Also,  she reports to having no fevers, chills or night sweats. She does report  to having a stinging pain in her right breast every so often.    SOCIAL HISTORY: She is a human resource consultant. She has one son. She  is married. She lives with her husband and her mother-in-law. Today she  is accompanied by her husband and her mother.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: VITAL SIGNS: Temperature 36, blood pressure 142/75,  pulse 74, respiratory rate 16, height 171.5 cm, weight 84.1 kg. GENERAL:  She is a well-appearing female in no acute distress with a KPS of 100%.  HEENT: Extraocular muscles are intact, pupils are equally round and  reactive to light, sclerae are clear, her oropharynx is clear. NECK:  Supple with no thyromegaly. BACK: Nontender. FLANK: Nontender. LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation and percussion bilaterally. HEART EXAM: Regular  rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs or gallops. She has no JVD and her  pulses are full. NODAL EXAM: No palpable cervical, axillary, supra or  infraclavicular or inguinal nodes are found. BREAST EXAM: The patient has  C-cup breasts which are symmetrical, have good contour. There are no skin  discolorations on either breast, no dimpling, no edema. Her nipples are  everted bilaterally. The patient reports to an area of occasional  scaliness on her left nipple which she says secretes clear liquid once and  a while. Today we cannot see it. Today her left nipple looks normal and  there are no visible secretions from the left or the right nipple. There  is no edema of her areolas bilaterally. On palpation, no nodules or masses  can be felt in the right or left breast in any quadrant. No nodules or  masses can be palpated under the nipple and areolar complex bilaterally.  ABDOMEN: Soft, nontender, not distended, good bowel sounds, no  organomegaly,  no palpable masses. EXTREMITIES: No cyanosis, clubbing or  edema. SKIN: Warm, dry and clear. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM: Cranial nerves  II-XII are grossly intact. Her sensation is intact and her motor strength  is 5/5 bilaterally.    LABORATORY DATA: None.    RADIOGRAPHS REVIEWED IN CLINIC: Our radiologist reviewed her CT scans and  found that she does have a stable lung nodule in the superior segment of  her right lower lobe and her right lung.    IMPRESSION AND PLAN: Mrs. Thaler is a 48-year-old premenopausal female  with newly diagnosed high-grade DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ) by core  needle biopsy done on 05/25/2003. She comes to Breast Tumor Board today  to discuss her options for therapy and to gain a better understanding of  her disease. After discussing her case in Tumor Board, Dr. Zavier Lew,  Surgery Department, discussed therapy options with the patient as well as  answered all of her questions. Dr. Nickell explained to Mrs. Teixeira that  her next step should be to undergo surgical excision of the cancer in her  right breast. The core biopsy showed about 1 cm of total DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) in scattered foci but no invasive component. With  surgery, if no invasive cancer is found, she has over a 90% recurrence-free  survival, that is if she chooses one of two options. The first is to have  a simple mastectomy of her right breast. The second option is to have  breast conservation therapy of her right breast which includes a lumpectomy  of all suspicious tissue on mammogram and all suspicious tissue as the  pathologists can see based on core biopsy samples, as well as any  suspicious tissue found during surgery; with clear margins of more then 2  mm. Here at Stanford, we do not recommend doing sentinel node biopsy on  patients with her profile. After the result of the pathologic analysis of  the removed breast tissues have been obtained after her surgery, we  discussed with the patient that we would  at that point do a followup and  plan on a course of action accordingly. Mrs. Teixeira expressed her wish of  having breast conservation therapy. If she has only DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) with no invasive cancer component with clear margins of  more than 2 mm on pathology then we would follow her in clinic with  frequent doctor visits including physical exam and yearly mammogram. Mrs.  Teixeira has an appointment to see Dr. Aiyden Dietzel in our Surgery  Department here at Mooresville  next week in order to discuss her  surgery. Should Mrs. Teixeira have any questions or concerns, Dr. Pasch  gave her her business card and advised her to call. The patient was also  given the California State Pamphlet on Breast Cancer. This patient was  also seen with Dr. Eulalia Jabeen, Oncology Fellow, and medical student  Olivencia, acting as scribe for Dr. Esser.        Forehand, M.D.  Oncology Department        Kent Nowels, M.D.  Pathology Department.        Jullian Zapp, M.D.  Radiology Department        Hector Zier, M.D.  Surgery Department            Khattak, Cascio, M.S.    cc: Horton, M.D.   Diagnostic Radiology   H1307/MC 641 Zygmont Ln, Greenwich CT      Vessely, M.D.   1000 Welch Road   Suite 599 4-H Camp Rd, Hedgesville WV      Lenihan, M.D.   300 Placerville , MC: 35 Azad Ct, Providence RI      Kent Nowels, M.D.   Pathology   Room H-491 113th Pl, Palos Hills IL       Mak, M.D.  300 Lufkin , MC: 437 Luman Rd, Quanah TX     d: 06/19/2003  t: 06/20/2003/drc  i: 06/20/2003/drc    doc: [000000]  15-1115

gold_text: IDENTIFICATION: The patient is a 54-year-old woman who has had a lifelong  feeling that her right hip was subluxating on her, but actually never came  out. It did not cause her too much pain except for maybe an instant. Her  chief complaint now is that she has right hip pain that has lasted three  months every time she swings her right hip into abduction and marked  external rotation, and she feels the pain on the lateral side of her hip,  although it is deep.    PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Her past history includes the fact that for several  years in the mid-1980s she was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis with  poly-symmetrical pain and inflammation as well as some swelling of the  hands, wrists, knees and at least one other set of joints. That went away  suddenly after being there for several years and has not returned since.  The last three months of having this pain is unique, because historically  she did not have pain other than for very brief moments. She also has a  past surgical history of removal of something from the anterior chest  superficially when she was either newly born or in the first few years of  life. She had cervical cancer for which she underwent surgery and she has  been free of any cancer since the operation which was in about 1987.  Otherwise, her past medical history of illnesses of the 14 that we checked  for were all negative.    ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.    MEDICATIONS: None.    SOCIAL HISTORY: She smokes one pack of cigarettes for 15 years but quit in  1996. She is an electrical engineer. She is married and lives with her  spouse.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: She has 12 systems that are negative. The only positive  ones are an occasional genitourinary infection and her musculoskeletal  problem.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: On physical examination she is a healthy appearing,  cooperative, energetic woman who is about 5'2" of normal weight. She has a  normal gait and she has full range of  motion in both hips, but on flexion  and marked external rotation with 90 degrees or +90 degrees of flexion can  create a problem that really causes her pain until she undoes this  position. This is definitely not over the greater trochanter, which is not  tender. There is no tenderness right over the site that is causing her  pain. There is no history of snapping, nor is there any on palpation. There  is no palpation pain to anywhere about her hip. The straight leg raise is  negative. Deep tendon reflexes are equal and symmetrical. There is normal  range of motion in her back both in flexion and extension as well as side  to side movements. There is no tenderness to palpation.    DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES: The x-rays of her hip, three views, AP, frog leg and  cross table are all normal.    IMPRESSION: The impression I have is probable labral tear of the right  hip.    PLAN:  1. We will send her to Dr. Lajuan Zeller, who is the only person I know  in the Kailua Kona  who does arthroscopy of the right hip.  2. I did not order an MRI because I believe that Dr. Asbury will do so.        Henley, M.D.    cc: Spangler J. Cosgrove, M.D.   R144/ MC 5326    d: 06/19/2001  t: 06/21/2001/hbt  i: 06/22/2001/tyl    doc: [000000]  15-1115

He reports since around 06/14/2021 he has had pain in his low bladder and pelvic area when he drinks water. Pain is worse when bladder is full, and pain improves after emptying.   Some increased urgency/frequency.  No incontinence, normal stream.  Denies sense of prostate pain or swelling.  Has pain in urethra with urination. If he drinks a lot of water, no dysuria, but if does not drink a lot of water has dysuria.  Occasional right testicular pain for months. He says he continues to have occasional very mild pain in right testicle if he touches it.  Had flank pain months ago, but this resolved.  Denies stone hx or hematuria.  He denies related trauma or injury.  Normal erections. No pain with ejaculation.    He thinks he had UTIs in the past, but seems he had no culture proven UTIs.    He was seen at the emergency room in Calhan  and was started on an unknown antibiotic in 06/14/2021 for suspected UTI or prostatitis.  He took the antibiotic for 2 weeks but had no improvement.  He was then switched to Levaquin 250mg daily for suspected prostatitis, which he took this for 2 weeks and then stopped, no improvement. He was evaluated at Salinas Valley Urology Associates a few times since 07/15/2021.  Started 500mg levaquin BID x 1-1.5 months but then had tendon pain and so he stopped it. Switched to bactrim for 1.5 months.    No improvement from abx other than mild improvement in his urethral pain with urination.  Has anxiety, has been stressed lately.  Stopped smoking marijuana usually "since it is not fun anymore."    Scrotal ultrasound 06/14/2021 showed normal testes and epididymis.  It did show a few right inguinal lymph nodes with fatty hila largest measuring 9  x 4 mm in cross-section.  No right inguinal hernia noted.    CT abdomen pelvis noncontrast on 06/14/2021 showed no acute intra-abdominal abnormality.  It did show a few prominent mesenteric lymph nodes.  And a few small right inguinal lymph nodes noted but otherwise was unremarkable of the right groin in the field-of-view.    Scrotal ultrasound 07/08/2021 showed normal ultrasound of the scrotum.  No evidence of intratesticular mass or torsion.    RBUS 08/28/2021 showed no hydro, no renal masses or stones. Bladder mostly empty, but no obvious bladder mass or stone seen.     Had a cystoscopy 09/14/2021 with Salinas Valley urology Associates and it reportedly showed no strictures or lesions. Reportedly was a normal cystoscopy.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS  UTI: denies culture proven UTIs  Hematuria: denies  Stones: denies  60 ounces water per day  Other fluids per day: no caffeine, no ETOH    Sexually active: he has never had sex. Never sexually active.  denies abnormal discharge or lesions. Denies any risk of STD. ASSESSMENT & PLAN  Patient with anxiety and with months of increased sense of urgency/frequency as well as bladder pain which is worse when full and better after emptied. Has months of dysuria and mild right testicular pain but no UTIs based on cultures, and no risk for STD (never sexually active). Flank pain which has resolved. Within the last few months he has had CT, scrotal US x 2, RBUS, and cysto which showed no cause. Imaging did show some lymph nodes of unclear significance. Was treated with multiple extended course abx for suspected prostatitis with little to no improvement (only mild dysuria improvement). He denies prostate swelling or prostate pain, and prostate normal on exam today.  PVR low today. Suspect he may have  OAB with painful bladder spasms, vs interstitial cystitis vs possible pelvic floor dysfunction. We discussed each of these and possible eval and treatment options. Low suspicion for prostatitis or UTI.     - Will get baseline labs - CBC and BMP. Also will send UA for micro to ensure no sig # of RBCs given trace on dip today. Suspect he may benefit from elavil, but will get UDS first  - Also recommend he consult with his PCP about the lymph node findings on imaging. gold_text: MEDICAL GENETICS CLINIC    ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Warden, MD  MEDICAL GENETICS FELLOW: Wynter Birk, MD  DATE OF CONSULTATION: 08/10/2017  SITE OF CONSULTATION: 730 Welch Road    REQUESTING PHYSICIAN: No Pcp    REASON FOR CONSULTATION: We are requested for initial consultation in Biochemical Genetics on Temperance Millstein regarding the diagnosis, management, and genetic counseling for the findings of chronic progressive external ophathlamoplegia (CPEO) mitochondrial disorder.      HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  Temperance is a 48-year old female with chronic progressive external ophathlamoplegia (CPEO) who came today to establish care for her mitochondrial disorder. She is from Oregon and used to be seen at OHSU Northwest Clinic, but for the last 10 years she was not seen by any metabolic doctor. She has travelled from Oregon last night to Rembert ,CA and is planning to return back today after our visit.      In 1987, she was diagnosed with chronic progressive external ophathlamoplegia (CPEO) with Janssen type lesion of mitochondrial DNA according to records. She reports that during pregnancy and early childhood everything was well. During second grade, she started having difficulty moving her eyes, which was corrected with glasses. At fifteen or sixteen years of age, she had severe headaches, and by 18 years old she had drooping eyelids. She was evaluated by ophthalmology at that time and was diagnosed with CPEO. She reported a history of muscle weakness and was always slow in running compared to her peers. She had exercise testing in the physiology laboratory where she showed early induction of anaerobic exercise. NMR spectroscopy in Vancouver was completed in Atwood , which showed an abnormal phosphocreatine to inorganic phosphate ratio. She was then treated with 3,000 mg nicotinamide per day and 300 mg riboflavin per day which reportedly improved the NMR spectroscopy results. At that time, they recommended  to follow up with GI for abdominal pain after feeding. Her cholesterol was reportedly high at 215, and many plasma amino acids below the normal range. Nicotinamide was reduced to 2,000 mg per day, Riboflavin increased to 600 mg per day, Vit C 4,000 mg per day, and Vit K 40mg per day. They recommended an open muscle biopsy. Her muscle biopsy showed ragged red fibers. Mitochondrial DNA analysis detected a deletion around 5kb in muscle mitochondrial DNA which is similar in size.     In 2003, she was diagnosed with velopharyngeal insufficiency, hypernasality and dysphagia by Dr. Franck. Barium swallow showed mild to moderate risk for aspiration.  In 2006, at Flintville , she had a voice evaluation, Laryngoscopy, Flowers, and Modified barium swallow which resulted with mild dysphonia and moderate dysphagia (associated with global weakness) and hypernasality. The reports showed trace penetration without frank aspiration. Per records, material passes down the left side of the oropharynx, which improves with the patient’s head turned to the right. She reports continued issue with swallowing and she feels the need to turn her head to the right.     In regarding her activity, she can walk 3-4 blocks before she will be out of breath or feels an ache in the back of her legs. Overall, she describe her symptoms as slow progression.  According to the patient, she reports never having any HbA1C evaluation or TSH screening labs.     MEDICAL HISTORY:  As detailed above in HPI and timeline below.     In 04/18/1989 at Oregon Health Sciences University. Open muscle biopsy done that showed ragged red fiber. Other workup as below.   -Oxygen uptake by isolated mitochondria: Normal substrate oxidation, consistent with normal electron transport chain activities   -Mitochondrial substrate oxidation: normal   -ETC activities in mitochondria: Within normal range; “This does not rule disorder in the ETC  associated with defect in mitochondria. There was a mitochondrial DNA deletion similar in size to deletion in patients with CPEO."  -Mitochondrial DNA analysis: Deletion around 5kb in muscle mitochondrial DNA     In 1990 at Las Vegas , she had a problem with periorbital tissue, with Levator Tarsus resection bilaterally by Dr. Paris Muenster.     In 11/18/1992, she had increasing diplopia, decreasing mobility in eyes, decreasing single field vision, and continued to have headaches. Headaches worsen with blurriness. She is able to exercise regularly. They recommend to start riboflabin 400mg/day, Co-enzyme Q 90mg TID total 270mg/day, and Vit C 3,000 mg /day. At that time, patient was not compliant with her vitamins, as she didn’t feel it aided her diplopia.     In 10/12/2003, she was diagnosed with velopharyngeal insufficiency, hypernasality and dysphagia by Dr. Maynor.     In 12/14/2004 OHSU Northwest Clinic for voice and swallowing study, she was found to have barium swallow and laryngyoscopy with mild to moderate risk for aspiration.  Palatal exercises were recommended at this time     In 12/10/2005, Dermatopathology analyzed shaved right breast 0.7 x 0.45cm lesion increasing in size. Diagnosis: melanocytic nevus, compound type.    In 05/10/2006 OHSU Northwest Clinic for voice and swallowing, she had a repeat barium swallow and complete evaluation of voice and swallowing with Speech Pathology and Laryngologist, Dr. Aidyn Workman.    In 09/14/2006 at Arapahoe , she had a oice evaluation, Laryngoscopy, Tejwani, and Modified barium swallow which resulted in mild dysphonia and moderate dysphagia (associated with global weakness) and hypernasality.Speech pathology submitted separate formal report - "Trace penetration without frank aspiration. Material passes down the left side of the oropharynx, which improves with the patient’s head turned to the  right."     In 10/21/2006 Facial Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery Clinic at University Hospital South, reported liquid escapes with swallowing, but its resolved if sitting while eating.   Evaluated for bilateral pharyngoplaties, palatal flap, or posteropharyngeal flap. Recommended that intervention will not be needed for a fair time.     In 01/10/2008, reported trouble with chewing, swallowing, speaking, eye movement and double vision.     In 05/09/2008 Facial plastic and reconstructive surgery clinic, which did not recommend surgery for brow or upper lid since the ptosis repair was not too long ago.     In 05/21/2009, she reported symptoms of sleep apnea and nocturnal hypoventilation.    In 11/14/2009, she reported weakness in lateral pharyngeal walls preventing closure of nasopharynx.  PFT: FVC 2.33L 61% predicted, FEV1 2.23 72% predicted     In 04/14/2011, she had the following evaluations.  -Voice evaluation: moderate dysphonia, articulation slightly abnormal   -Laryngoscopy: palatal weakness   -She has been losing weight over the past 12 to 18 months. Her approximate calorie intake at this time was around 1400 calories per day.   -Poorly tolerated Carnitor 500mg BID with nighttime reflux and heartburn; dose was changed to Carnitor 500mg in the morning     In 05/30/2012, she was diagnosed with Granuloma annulare and given clobetasol ointment.    In 06/15/2012, she reported more weakness in neck, tension headaches, dry eyes for which she wears patch at night.  She goes to the gym 2x/week and started Elavil for abdominal pain.    In 10/22/2006, she was seen in  OHSU Northwest Clinic for voice and swallowing.  She had a repeat barium swallow and voice evaluation: moderate dysphagia, hypernasal speech, velopharyngeal incompetence.  Discussed Custer and palatal lift prosthesis.    In 04/10/2013, she had studies with FVC 47% of predicted.    In 07/31/2013 CXRAY 2 views for history  of weakness and shortness of breath reported Impression: normal   Pulmonary Function: Moderate restrictive impairment by reduced FVC and normal FEV1/FVC   Pulmonary clinic recommended follow up for chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure. Patient underwent unsatisfactory polysomnography with AVAPS therapy prescribed.     In 09/03/2013, Polysomnography was performed which revealed sleep apnea related to neuromuscular dysfunction.    In 10/09/2013, recommended cough assist device use 2x/day, follow up with sleep specialist.    In 10/12/2013, seen in Neuromuscular Clinic and reported takes Coenzyme Q10, 300mg every other day.  PEG tube was discussed; patient declined.     In 10/20/2016, she started going to Summit Family Medicine. She was diagnosed with fatigue, infection of left toe, and generalized neuromuscular exhaustion syndrome.    In 11/17/2016, she was diagnosed with Cellulitis of left middle toenail.  In 11/29/2016, she had yearly EKG checkup.  In 02/02/2017, stopping BCPs due to racing heart.  In 03/08/2017, she had labs at Mid Columbia Medical Center   -  Platelet count 131 (low); MPV 9.1 (low); Creatinine plasma 0.3 (low)   In 04/18/2017, she had syncopal episode follow up   In 05/17/2017, she had cellulitis of the left middle toe; was prescribed Bactrim 400mg-80mg PO tab 1x/per day for 10 days     DEVELOPMENT:  Ms. Millstein reports that she gained all her milestones on time and finished her 4 years in college.       REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:   A complete review of systems was performed; all systems are negative, except as documented in HPI and PMH.    Medications:    No outpatient prescriptions have been marked as taking for the 08/10/2017 encounter (Office Visit) with Butts, MD.   As discussed above in HPI.    Allergies:  Allergies   Allergen Reactions   • Lidocaine       Per patient    • Penicillins      Per patient       FAMILY HISTORY:  There are no other family members with CPEO  diagnosis, intellectual disability, birth defects, or genetic conditions.  Consanguinity was denied. Please see below 3-generation pedigree for further details.      SOCIAL HISTORY: Temperance lives at 662 System Dr, Huntington Beach CA     MEASUREMENTS:  Vitals Last Set (from any encounter) 08/10/2017   BP 122/81   Weight 40.5 kg   Height 165.9 cm   Head Cir 54.5 cm   BMI 14.75 kg/m2        Physical Exam   Constitutional: She is oriented to person, place, and time.   Noted for thin body habitus     HENT:   Head: Normocephalic.   Nose: Nose normal.   Eyes: Right eye exhibits abnormal extraocular motion. Left eye exhibits abnormal extraocular motion.   Bilateral ptosis and her eye fixed in primary gaze, ophthalmoplegia ( was unable to move her eyes)   Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds.    Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal.   Abdominal: Soft. Bowel sounds are normal.   Musculoskeletal: Normal range of motion.   Neurological: She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. She has normal reflexes.   Proximal muscle weakness     Skin: Skin is dry.   Psychiatric: She has a normal mood and affect.       IMPRESSION:  Sydney Vieira is a 48-year old female with Chronic Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) who came to our clinic to establish new care. She is very interested in the upcoming clinical trial for adult patient with underlying mitochondrial disorder.     1. Mitochondrial myopathy  Amino Acids, Plasma    Lactate, WB    Organic Acids, Urine    Carnitine Level Free and Total    Hemoglobin A1c    Acylcarnitine Profile Quant    Creatine Kinase, Total    Metabolic Panel, Comprehensive     DISCUSSION:   We initiated our discussion by reviewing the role of the mitochondria in energy metabolism. Mitochondria are the parts of our cells that produce the majority of the cell's energy. There are two sets of genes, DNA instructions, that are used to make the  mitochondria. The majority of these genes are inherited from both parents and found in the nucleus. A smaller subset of genes is located in a circular DNA molecule within the mitochondria itself, which is passed only from the mother to her children.     Large-scale single deletions of mtDNA are associated with three distinct phenotypes: Pearson marrow-pancreas syndrome (PS), Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS), and Chronic Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia Syndrome (PEO). These phenotypes form a continuum of disease. However; the clinical heterogeneity of mtDNA deletion syndrome is enormous ranging from mild to more severe debilitated spectrum.     CPEO/KSS are mitochondrial diseases where there is a piece of DNA material deleted  from the mitochondrial DNA. They may be inherited or passed down from one or both parents, but they are often caused by a spontaneous DNA mutation. Signs and symptoms tend to begin in early adulthood and most commonly include weakness or paralysis of the muscles that move the eye and drooping of the eyelids (ptosis).Some affected individuals also have general weakness, which may be especially noticeable during exercise. Muscle weakness may also cause difficulty swallowing.     Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) can be inherited, or it can occur sporadically (due to a new mutation in an individual with no history of the condition in the family). Temperance Millstein had a deletion around 5kb in muscle mitochondrial DNA per report similar in size to individuals with CPEO.     CPEO is considered a "mitochondrial disorder." This is because all the genetic mutations that can cause CPEO ultimately result in dysfunction of the mitochondria, which are structures in our cells that produce energy required for normal cell function. It presents with ocular involvement including weakness of the eye muscles which can also be accompanied by myopathy or exercise intolerance. Other reported symptoms  include cardiac findings, dysphagia, hearing loss, neuropathy, ataxia, and other associated findings for which multidisciplinary care is recommended.  In general, we also recommend routinely screening for endocrine conditions or diabetes mellitus in mitochondrial conditions. Therefore, we recommend surveillance and monitoring by multiple subspecialists for comprehensive care.    Time was spent discussing that although there is no specific treatment for mitochondrial disorders, supplements like CoQ10, alpha lipoic acid, and vitamin B are recommended according to consensus treatment guidelines. We typically recommend one vitamin B50 tablet twice daily except for B1 (it would be 100 mg daily v. 250 mg daily for B1), and would provide some other B vitamins that may help. According to the consensus treatment guideline, specific dosages are not given, so we really do not have an "optimal" treatment dosage recommendation. However, based on the medical literature, the following dosages have been used in adults:     ·  Coenzyme Q10 - 150 mg to 300 mg daily (We recommend the Tishcon or Vitaline brand) - Coenzyme Q10 comes in 2 forms, "ubiquinone" and "ubiquinol." The ubiquinol form is considered to be more highly bioavailable (better absorbed and distributed through the body), so lower dosages are needed compared to ubiquinone. Typically we recommend following the manufacturer's instructions (e.g. for Tishcon ubiquinol the dosage is 2 soft capsules daily, which provides 200 mg of active coenzyme Q10). There are a multitude of preparations available (e.g., gels, capsules, tablets, etc.), and we do not have a specific preference.   ·  Vitamin B50 - 1 tablet twice daily  ·  Alpha-lipoic acid - 300 mg daily    Certain medications and anesthetic agents are contraindicated for mitochondrial disorders. Clinical necessity sometimes requires use of these, however, we recommend choosing alternatives if possible.     Today we will check  lactic acid, plasma amino acids, urine organic acids, acylcarnitine profile and free and total carnitine as these are useful markers for disease activity and treatment monitoring. Time was spent discussing that there are upcoming clinical trials for mitochondrial disorders. We also discussed the availability of NAMDC registry which Ms. Millstein agreed to participate in.  Time was spent discussing the upcoming clinical trials for mitochondrial disorders as Temperance Millstein met with our research coordinator.     It was a pleasure to see Temperance today.  We would like to see Temperance back in metabolic clinic in 12 months.  Should any questions or concerns arise following today’s visit, we encourage the family to contact the Genetics Office at 999-999-9999 during business hours.      RECOMMENDATIONS/PLAN:  1) Obtain biochemical genetics labs (lactate, plasma amino acids, carnitine level, acylcarnitine profile, urine organic acids).  We will check CK and HbA1C.  2) We discussed mitochondrial supplements CoQ10, alpha lipoic acid, and vitamin B50. We provided information regarding the consensus guidelines.  3) A mitochondrial anesthesia protocol should be used if she undergoes any procedure requiring anesthesia.   4) Continue close follow up with multiple subspecialists is beneficial including Cardiology, ENT, Pulmonology, GI/Nutrition, Ophthalmology, Audiology, and Neurology.  5) Ms. Millstein met with our research coordinators for NAMDC registry and to answer questions about upcoming clinical trials.  6) Return to clinic in 1 year, sooner prn concerns.    Teaching Physician Attestation   I saw and examined the patient and discussed her management with the resident/fellow. I reviewed the resident/fellow's note, edited as needed, and agree with the documented findings and plan of care.   Flores, MD

IMPRESSION:  Sydney Vieira is a 48-year old female with Chronic Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) who came to our clinic to establish new care. She is very interested in the upcoming clinical trial for adult patient with underlying mitochondrial disorder.     1. Mitochondrial myopathy  Amino Acids, Plasma    Lactate, WB    Organic Acids, Urine    Carnitine Level Free and Total    Hemoglobin A1c    Acylcarnitine Profile Quant    Creatine Kinase, Total    Metabolic Panel, Comprehensive     DISCUSSION:   We initiated our discussion by reviewing the role of the mitochondria in energy metabolism. Mitochondria are the parts of our cells that produce the majority of the cell's energy. There are two sets of genes, DNA instructions, that are used to make the  mitochondria. The majority of these genes are inherited from both parents and found in the nucleus. A smaller subset of genes is located in a circular DNA molecule within the mitochondria itself, which is passed only from the mother to her children.     Large-scale single deletions of mtDNA are associated with three distinct phenotypes: Pearson marrow-pancreas syndrome (PS), Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS), and Chronic Progressive External Ophthalmoplegia Syndrome (PEO). These phenotypes form a continuum of disease. However; the clinical heterogeneity of mtDNA deletion syndrome is enormous ranging from mild to more severe debilitated spectrum.     CPEO/KSS are mitochondrial diseases where there is a piece of DNA material deleted  from the mitochondrial DNA. They may be inherited or passed down from one or both parents, but they are often caused by a spontaneous DNA mutation. Signs and symptoms tend to begin in early adulthood and most commonly include weakness or paralysis of the muscles that move the eye and drooping of the eyelids (ptosis).Some affected individuals also have general weakness, which may be especially noticeable during exercise. Muscle weakness may also cause difficulty swallowing.     Chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO) can be inherited, or it can occur sporadically (due to a new mutation in an individual with no history of the condition in the family). Temperance Millstein had a deletion around 5kb in muscle mitochondrial DNA per report similar in size to individuals with CPEO.     CPEO is considered a "mitochondrial disorder." This is because all the genetic mutations that can cause CPEO ultimately result in dysfunction of the mitochondria, which are structures in our cells that produce energy required for normal cell function. It presents with ocular involvement including weakness of the eye muscles which can also be accompanied by myopathy or exercise intolerance. Other reported symptoms  include cardiac findings, dysphagia, hearing loss, neuropathy, ataxia, and other associated findings for which multidisciplinary care is recommended.  In general, we also recommend routinely screening for endocrine conditions or diabetes mellitus in mitochondrial conditions. Therefore, we recommend surveillance and monitoring by multiple subspecialists for comprehensive care.    Time was spent discussing that although there is no specific treatment for mitochondrial disorders, supplements like CoQ10, alpha lipoic acid, and vitamin B are recommended according to consensus treatment guidelines. We typically recommend one vitamin B50 tablet twice daily except for B1 (it would be 100 mg daily v. 250 mg daily for B1), and would provide some other B vitamins that may help. According to the consensus treatment guideline, specific dosages are not given, so we really do not have an "optimal" treatment dosage recommendation. However, based on the medical literature, the following dosages have been used in adults:     ·  Coenzyme Q10 - 150 mg to 300 mg daily (We recommend the Tishcon or Vitaline brand) - Coenzyme Q10 comes in 2 forms, "ubiquinone" and "ubiquinol." The ubiquinol form is considered to be more highly bioavailable (better absorbed and distributed through the body), so lower dosages are needed compared to ubiquinone. Typically we recommend following the manufacturer's instructions (e.g. for Tishcon ubiquinol the dosage is 2 soft capsules daily, which provides 200 mg of active coenzyme Q10). There are a multitude of preparations available (e.g., gels, capsules, tablets, etc.), and we do not have a specific preference.   ·  Vitamin B50 - 1 tablet twice daily  ·  Alpha-lipoic acid - 300 mg daily    Certain medications and anesthetic agents are contraindicated for mitochondrial disorders. Clinical necessity sometimes requires use of these, however, we recommend choosing alternatives if possible.     Today we will check  lactic acid, plasma amino acids, urine organic acids, acylcarnitine profile and free and total carnitine as these are useful markers for disease activity and treatment monitoring. Time was spent discussing that there are upcoming clinical trials for mitochondrial disorders. We also discussed the availability of NAMDC registry which Ms. Millstein agreed to participate in.  Time was spent discussing the upcoming clinical trials for mitochondrial disorders as Temperance Millstein met with our research coordinator.     It was a pleasure to see Temperance today.  We would like to see Temperance back in metabolic clinic in 12 months.  Should any questions or concerns arise following today’s visit, we encourage the family to contact the Genetics Office at 999-999-9999 during business hours. We will check CK and HbA1C.  2) We discussed mitochondrial supplements CoQ10, alpha lipoic acid, and vitamin B50. We provided information regarding the consensus guidelines.  3) A mitochondrial anesthesia protocol should be used if she undergoes any procedure requiring anesthesia.   4) Continue close follow up with multiple subspecialists is beneficial including Cardiology, ENT, Pulmonology, GI/Nutrition, Ophthalmology, Audiology, and Neurology.  5) Ms. Millstein met with our research coordinators for NAMDC registry and to answer questions about upcoming clinical trials.  6) Return to clinic in 1 year, sooner prn concerns. If she becomes ill with symptoms such as poor appetite, fever, vomiting, diarrhea, or lethargy, she is to be evaluated urgently (within ½ hour).  Please follow the steps, below:     1. Establish vascular access and administer dextrose-containing fluids and electrolytes appropriate for age     2. Obtain the following labs (in addition to any other appropriate testing):  • Lactate (drawn free-flowing, without tourniquet)  • CMP  • CBC w/ Diff      3. The following medications should be avoided in patients with mitochondrial disease when possible and, if given, they should be used with caution:   • Lactated ringers  • Valproic acid  • Statins  • Metformin  • High-dose acetaminophen   • Selected antibiotics, including aminoglycosides, linezolid, tetracycline, azithromycin, and erythromycin.    4. Mitochondrial patients often have adverse reactions to certain anesthetic agents, so if sedation or anesthesia are required please refer to our separate anesthesia protocol. Troy has a diagnosis of a mitochondrial disorder due to mitochondrial DNA deletion syndrome.  Many anesthetic agents can affect mitochondrial function and patients with mitochondrial disorders may be uniquely sensitive to many anesthetic agents. Therefore, we recommend the following precautions including:    1. A preoperative anesthesia consult to assess possible increased sensitivity to certain anesthetics and as well as the patient’s many possible comorbidities.    2. It is recommended that the patient be the first case of the day to minimize fasting intervals, and Shlomo Heron should be given dextrose-containing IV fluids with appropriate electrolytes for age while NPO.     3. Anesthetic agents with known mitochondrial toxicity as well as lactated Ringer’s solution should be avoided    4. Patients may require prolonged post-operative monitoring    5. Since each case is unique we recommend contacting our anesthesiology team with expertise in mitochondrial disorders at 999-999-9999 to discuss Troy’s case. HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS: Mrs. Zapata has been followed by yearly  mammograms. On a recent mammogram done on 05/28/2003, an abnormality  was found. A development of a 1 cm area of heterogeneous  microcalcifications located in the right breast at the 7 o'clock position  in the lower outer quadrant located 9 cm from the nipple. The patient was  advised to have that area biopsied as it was suspicious for malignancy. Of  note, there are no suspicious lesions in her left breast. On 06/04/2003, the patient underwent core needle biopsy of the suspicious area in  her right breast. The pathology report showed diffuse patches of  high-grade DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ), that measured 2 mm in greatest  dimension, solid and comedo type, with microcalcifications, located at the  7 o'clock position in the right breast, 9 cm from the nipple. No ER/PR  staining was done at that time.
The patient has a lung nodule measuring approximately 1.7 x 1.5 cm in the  superior segment of the right lower lobe of her lung. It was discovered in  1999. The patient reports that it was biopsied in 09/31/1999 and  although we do not have a report of the biopsy the patient tells us that it  was diagnosed as a benign hamartoma. We do have chest CT scan reports  dating from 1999 yearly to a recent one done on 06/18/2003, all of which  describe this nodule as remaining stable. It has not grown or changed in  approximately four years. Today, Mrs. Zapata presents to Breast Tumor  Board in order to discuss her therapy options and to obtain  recommendations.

SMOKING: From 1975 to 2000 the patient smoked about one cigarette per day.  She no longer smokes.    ALCOHOL: Two drinks per week, mostly wine.    ENVIRONMENTAL EXPOSURES: None.    RADIATION EXPOSURE: None.    FAMILY HISTORY: Maternal grandmother with breast cancer diagnosed when she  was 75 years old. She had a mastectomy. She had cancer in only one  breast. Her father is age 77, alive and has myasthenia  gravis. The patient has two sisters, one age 46 and one age 47. One has  benign uterine growth which the patient cannot describe other than tumors  which sometimes need to be removed.    PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  1. Hypertension.  2. Chronic UTI (urinary tract infection) starting at age 9, status post  V-plasty.  3. Status post car accident with multiple surgeries now with chronic   neck and back problems.  4. History of hepatitis A and mononucleosis.  5. History of basal cell carcinomas which have been excised.    ALLERGIES: Penicillin, anaphylactic shock when takes penicillin.    MEDICATIONS: Avapro and lisinopril. She does report  to having a stinging pain in her right breast every so often.    SOCIAL HISTORY: She is a human resource consultant. She has one son. She  is married. HEENT: Extraocular muscles are intact, pupils are equally round and  reactive to light, sclerae are clear, her oropharynx is clear. NECK:  Supple with no thyromegaly. BACK: Nontender. FLANK: Nontender. LUNGS:  Clear to auscultation and percussion bilaterally. HEART EXAM: Regular  rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs or gallops. She has no JVD and her  pulses are full. NODAL EXAM: No palpable cervical, axillary, supra or  infraclavicular or inguinal nodes are found. BREAST EXAM: The patient has  C-cup breasts which are symmetrical, have good contour. There are no skin  discolorations on either breast, no dimpling, no edema. Her nipples are  everted bilaterally. The patient reports to an area of occasional  scaliness on her left nipple which she says secretes clear liquid once and  a while. Today we cannot see it. Today her left nipple looks normal and  there are no visible secretions from the left or the right nipple. There  is no edema of her areolas bilaterally. On palpation, no nodules or masses  can be felt in the right or left breast in any quadrant. No nodules or  masses can be palpated under the nipple and areolar complex bilaterally.  ABDOMEN: Soft, nontender, not distended, good bowel sounds, no  organomegaly,  no palpable masses. EXTREMITIES: No cyanosis, clubbing or  edema. SKIN: Warm, dry and clear. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM: Cranial nerves  II-XII are grossly intact. Her sensation is intact and her motor strength  is 5/5 bilaterally.    LABORATORY DATA: None.    RADIOGRAPHS REVIEWED IN CLINIC: Our radiologist reviewed her CT scans and  found that she does have a stable lung nodule in the superior segment of  her right lower lobe and her right lung.    IMPRESSION AND PLAN: Mrs. Thaler is a 48-year-old premenopausal female  with newly diagnosed high-grade DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ) by core  needle biopsy done on 05/25/2003. She comes to Breast Tumor Board today  to discuss her options for therapy and to gain a better understanding of  her disease. After discussing her case in Tumor Board, Dr. Zavier Lew,  Surgery Department, discussed therapy options with the patient as well as  answered all of her questions. Dr. Nickell explained to Mrs. Teixeira that  her next step should be to undergo surgical excision of the cancer in her  right breast. The core biopsy showed about 1 cm of total DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) in scattered foci but no invasive component. With  surgery, if no invasive cancer is found, she has over a 90% recurrence-free  survival, that is if she chooses one of two options. The first is to have  a simple mastectomy of her right breast. The second option is to have  breast conservation therapy of her right breast which includes a lumpectomy  of all suspicious tissue on mammogram and all suspicious tissue as the  pathologists can see based on core biopsy samples, as well as any  suspicious tissue found during surgery; with clear margins of more then 2  mm. Here at Stanford, we do not recommend doing sentinel node biopsy on  patients with her profile. After the result of the pathologic analysis of  the removed breast tissues have been obtained after her surgery, we  discussed with the patient that we would  at that point do a followup and  plan on a course of action accordingly. Mrs. Teixeira expressed her wish of  having breast conservation therapy. If she has only DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) with no invasive cancer component with clear margins of  more than 2 mm on pathology then we would follow her in clinic with  frequent doctor visits including physical exam and yearly mammogram. Mrs.  Teixeira has an appointment to see Dr. Aiyden Dietzel in our Surgery  Department here at Mooresville  next week in order to discuss her  surgery. Should Mrs. Teixeira have any questions or concerns, Dr. Pasch  gave her her business card and advised her to call. The patient was also  given the California State Pamphlet on Breast Cancer. This patient was  also seen with Dr. Eulalia Jabeen, Oncology Fellow, and medical student  Olivencia, acting as scribe for Dr. Esser. Today her left nipple looks normal and  there are no visible secretions from the left or the right nipple. There  is no edema of her areolas bilaterally. On palpation, no nodules or masses  can be felt in the right or left breast in any quadrant. No nodules or  masses can be palpated under the nipple and areolar complex bilaterally.  ABDOMEN: Soft, nontender, not distended, good bowel sounds, no  organomegaly,  no palpable masses. EXTREMITIES: No cyanosis, clubbing or  edema. SKIN: Warm, dry and clear. NEUROLOGICAL EXAM: Cranial nerves  II-XII are grossly intact. Her sensation is intact and her motor strength  is 5/5 bilaterally.    LABORATORY DATA: None.    RADIOGRAPHS REVIEWED IN CLINIC: Our radiologist reviewed her CT scans and  found that she does have a stable lung nodule in the superior segment of  her right lower lobe and her right lung.    IMPRESSION AND PLAN: Mrs. Thaler is a 48-year-old premenopausal female  with newly diagnosed high-grade DCIS (ductal carcinoma-in-situ) by core  needle biopsy done on 05/25/2003. She comes to Breast Tumor Board today  to discuss her options for therapy and to gain a better understanding of  her disease. After discussing her case in Tumor Board, Dr. Zavier Lew,  Surgery Department, discussed therapy options with the patient as well as  answered all of her questions. Dr. Nickell explained to Mrs. Teixeira that  her next step should be to undergo surgical excision of the cancer in her  right breast. The core biopsy showed about 1 cm of total DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) in scattered foci but no invasive component. With  surgery, if no invasive cancer is found, she has over a 90% recurrence-free  survival, that is if she chooses one of two options. The first is to have  a simple mastectomy of her right breast. The second option is to have  breast conservation therapy of her right breast which includes a lumpectomy  of all suspicious tissue on mammogram and all suspicious tissue as the  pathologists can see based on core biopsy samples, as well as any  suspicious tissue found during surgery; with clear margins of more then 2  mm. Here at Stanford, we do not recommend doing sentinel node biopsy on  patients with her profile. After the result of the pathologic analysis of  the removed breast tissues have been obtained after her surgery, we  discussed with the patient that we would  at that point do a followup and  plan on a course of action accordingly. Mrs. Teixeira expressed her wish of  having breast conservation therapy. If she has only DCIS (ductal  carcinoma-in-situ) with no invasive cancer component with clear margins of  more than 2 mm on pathology then we would follow her in clinic with  frequent doctor visits including physical exam and yearly mammogram. Mrs.  Teixeira has an appointment to see Dr. Aiyden Dietzel in our Surgery  Department here at Mooresville  next week in order to discuss her  surgery. Should Mrs. Teixeira have any questions or concerns, Dr. Pasch  gave her her business card and advised her to call. The patient was also  given the California State Pamphlet on Breast Cancer. This patient was  also seen with Dr. Eulalia Jabeen, Oncology Fellow, and medical student  Olivencia, acting as scribe for Dr. Esser. gold_text: Stanford Center for Movement Disorders    12/25/2013    Sunio, Sol Hedin, MD  Visalia Medical Clinic  947 X48 Rd, Placerville CO     RE:    Norris, Burnell#   [000000]  DOB  02/25/1944    Dear Dr.  Sunio.,    We have the pleasure of seeing Mr. Krysta Norris, a 69 Y R-handed male in consultation at the Stanford Center for Memory Disorders for possible Alzheimer's Disease.     Mr. Norris presents today with his wife for further evaluation. Mr. Norris's wife, Kump, describes a 10-year slowly progressive memory decline. The patient notes his memory decline has been noticeable over the past 1-2 years. His memory difficulties are characterized by short-term memory deficits. He at times does not recall individuals' names or where he has left items. He also has difficulties recalling a list of tasks he should complete. He does not experience difficulties recalling where he parked his car or with spatial orientation. He notes no difficulties with parking. His mother reports that the patient repeats the same stories. His wife does not recall episodes of Mr. Norris repeating stories or asking questions repeatedly. He was started on donepezil and namenda (unclear sequence) over the past 1.5 years and notes that it halted his memory decline.    Of note, he has a longstanding history of forgetting appointments dating back to his young adulthood.    As regards other nonmemory symptoms:  He denies any significant word-finding trouble. He notes a postural tremor in his R hand that began 30 years prior. The tremor worsens with certain activities such as writing, drinking. He also has a family history of tremor. With bending forward to pick up a golf ball he notes feeling lightheaded though denies a similar experience when getting up from bed quickly. He and his wife deny falls, bradykinesia.    His wife and the patient note greater irritability typically related to difficulties recalling. His  threshold for frustration is lowering with responses increasing. The patient and his wife specifically deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm during frustrated episodes.     His wife notes excessive snoring in the evenings as well as jerking movements which shake the bed over the past 5 years. The movements can occur in all extremities and are characterized by a series of jerking movement that cease after a few seconds. The movements do not occur during the day. He notes multiple episodes of his jaws clamping down with associated tongue biting during his sleep, 2-3x/year. His wife denies that the patient acts out his dreams. The patient denies vivid dreams.     He reports occasional constipation that is not particularly problematic. He denies changes in his urinary habits. He reports erectile dysfunction beginning in his late 40s. Of note, he receives testosterone shots monthly for low testosterone.    As regards seizure risk factors, he denies a history of head trauma, meningitis/encephalitis, episodes of LOC, stroke, seizures.    He denies difficulties with activities of daily living including bathing, dressing, feeding himself. He manages his own medications. His wife pays the bills and always had, this is not a change.     PMH:  HLD  Skin cancer  Spasmodic dysphonia first discovered in 90s for which he receives botox injections  Back pain    PSH:  Rectal surgery    MEDS:  Testosterone  B12 1000mcg PO DAILY  Donepezil 10mg PO every other day  namenda 10mg PO BID  dexilant 60mg PO DAILY  Simvastatin 20mg PO QHS  Cyclobenzaprine 10mg PO BID  Tramadol ER 200mg PO DAILY  botox injections for spasmodic dysphonia  Hydrocodone/APAP 5/325 PO BID PRN  claritin  colace    Allergy: Review of patient's allergies indicates no known allergies.    SH: He previously worked for the Sheriff's Department and currently works in the local community college. He completed 2 years of college. He lives with his wife in  Garrison . He previously smoked for many years and quit several years ago. He chewed tobacco for 8 years which he quit several years ago. He drinks a glass of wine every evening.    FH: Father with colon cancer. No family history of dementia. Family members with tremor    Review of Systems: Pertinent positives are documented above. A 14-system review was performed and was otherwise unremarkable.       Review of previous medical records:    Physical Examination: BP 120/74 | Pulse 80 | Ht 1.829 m (6') | Wt 99.791 kg (220 lb) | BMI 29.83 kg/m2.  Lying BP 130/79, HR 60  Standing 122/70, HR 72  He appears well and comfortable. Head is normocephalic and atraumatic, oral pharynx is clear without lesions, mucous membranes moist, no cervical lymphadenopathy, neck is supple without rigidity, limbs are warm, well perfused, without obvious skin lesions and without edema.     MS: He is attentive, oriented, and has normal memory and fund of knowledge. Speech is raspy though comprehensible. Language is fluent in expression with intact ability to follow complex commands and answer questions appropriately.   MOCA was completed, with a score of 26/30 with points deducted for word recall.   CN: PERRL, EOMI, face symmetric in strength and sensation, palate raises symmetrically, tongue midline, SCM and trapezius full in strength  Motor: Normal bulk. Postural tremor of RUE>LUE. No resting tremor. Mildly increased tone with activation though no baseline rigidity. No cogwheeling. Intact and symmetric finger / foot taps. Full strength throughout.  Sensation: Intact to light touch throughout  Coordination: FNF intact, HKS intact  Gait: Fluid and normal with intact heel and toe walking, and intact and normal tandem gait. There is no Romberg sign.      Data: Head CT from 12/12 reviewed. No significant atrophy or infarcts.    Impression:  Mr. Shank is a 69 y/o M with a history of HLD presenting for further evaluation of memory deficits  beginning 10 years prior though most prominent over the past 1 to 2 years. His memory deficits are characterized by short-term recall deficits. These deficits are accompanied by frustration. His physical exam is notable for a raspy voice and postural tremor. Work-up thus far includes normal B12 and head CT that is unremarkable. His symptoms are consistent with mild cognitive impairment. He does not exhibit any prominent signs of Parkinsonism (he may have essential tremor) or report symptoms concerning for Lewy Body Dementia. To further assess his mild cognitive impairment we recommend evaluation with an MRI Brain, neuropsychological testing, and checking TSH, RPR. As regards medication recommendations, we recommend minimizing the use of sedating medications such as cyclobenzaprine, tramadol, and vicodin. We would also recommend discontinuing namenda as this can cause confusion and is indicated for moderate-severe dementia. It is possible that a good component of his cognitive trouble is medication related.    As regards his wife's complaints of sleep disturbance, this is characterized by jerking movements during sleep. The semiology of the events is not suggestive of REM behavior disorder and more likely myoclonic jerking during sleep. However, given that we do not have a clear description of the events we would like to obtain a spot EEG to assess for ictal/interictal activity.    The patient has a postural tremor, likely an essential tremor given his family history and the quality of the tremor. The patient currently denies any disturbance as a result of this tremor, therefore, we would not recommend any medication management at this time.     The patient's frustrated episodes in relation to his memory deficits were discussed at length. His wife reports some concern that these may worsen though the patient and his wife deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm. The patient does have guns at home which his  wife assures us will be removed from the home when she insists upon it. We have counseled the patient's wife to call 911 should she be concerned about the possibility of physical harm. She understand this plan. The patient additionally understands that he should monitor his frustration.    Recommendations:  -We would recommend checking TSH, RPR to check reversible causes of cognitive impairement  -We will obtain an MRI 3T Memory Protocol  -We will check a spot EEG to evaluate for ictal/interictal activity given jerking movements during sleep with tongue biting  -We will order Neuropsychological testing to further evaluate cognitive impairment  -As regards medication adjustments, we have recommended tapering off of namenda. In addition, we have recommended minimizing the use of sedating medications including cyclobenzaprine, vicodin, and tramadol as these may further impair memory.  -Continue donepezil at this time  -RTC in 7 months    Thank you for the referral.    Sincerely,    Maris, MD   1:28 PM, 12/25/2013    I examined and discussed the patient with Dr. Centanni.  I have edited her excellent note and now agree with it in its entirety.  Townsend, MD  Assistant Professor

Telehealth:  GEN: appears well, in no apparent distress, pleasant and cooperative   HEENT: EOMI, conjunctiva clear   RESP: breathing comfortably, speaking in full sentences, no tachypnea  NEURO: alert and oriented, CN grossly intact, moves upper extremities appropriately  SKIN: normal skin appearance  PSYCH: appropriate affect, intact thought and speech    Assessment and Plan: 32 Y G0 presents for an endometriosis consult. S/p a mini-lap for a R 15cm endometrioma in 2012. Has been on COCs since then. Overall asymptomatic.    Questions today about endometriosis, cancer risk (mother diagnosed with stage 4 endometrial CA) and fertility.     Pt previously followed by Dr. Hildebrand at UCSF - wants serial US with AFCs.     Plan outlined with pt:    Endometriosis is a chronic disease that requires life-long management with the goal of maximizing medical treatment and avoiding repeated surgery     There are different treatment options for endometriosis:    - Wait and watch  - Medical treatment: birth control (pill, patch, ring, Mirena IUD, Depo Provera injection), Lupron (1 month or 3 month injection, causes a temporary menopause, use up to 1 year), Ponnuru (daily pill, also causes a temporary menopause, use either for 6 months or 2 years)  - Surgical treatment: conservative (laparoscopy to treat endometriosis), definitive (hysterectomy with or without removal of ovaries)    Surgery does entail more risk than medical and non-invasive therapy.  The risks include: bleeding, infection, injury to internal organs (including but not limited to: bowel, bladder, ureters, blood vessels), need for further surgery, and need for laparotomy. There is no guarantee that surgery will alleviate your symptoms. There is the risk of persistent, worse and/or new onset pain.    Plan:  - Continue birth control pills for now continuously.  - Please send your mother's genetic testing results, also your operative report.  - Per your preference for ultrasounds to monitor cysts and follicle counts, refer to Stanford Fertility Clinic, Dr. Phillip Prescott. He can also discuss egg freezing with you. The phone number is 999-999-9999. Please see if he will do your well woman care.    Return to clinic as needed.     Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. All of her questions were answered to her satisfaction.     (V) Face-to-face time with the patient: 60 minutes. (C) Counseling/Coordination of care: 40 minutes regarding above.    Please refer to the Assessment and Plan contents for details of what the patient was counseled on.    Susann Dobbins, MD  Clinical Assistant Professor   Division of Gynecology

gold_text: ID/CC: 40 year-old female with MS here to establish care.    HPI: Enrique Siu is a 40 year old right handed lady who has suffered from MS since 2000. Patient's first episode was tingling and numbness in her legs which lead to a work up for neuropathy. MRI scan of the brain and LP for CSF examination gave the diagnosis of MS. Since then patient has been treated with disease-modifying agent Copaxone. Her symptoms have been stable within the past couple of years. Patient never had relapses. She stated that she would one day notice she has some complaint that she did not have before.   Her current issues are  -back pain  -leg spasms  -fatigue  -urinary retention and incontinence  -memory issues  She was followed by Dr. Thornton of Point Hope . She recently moved to Killeen and would like to find a new neurologist to manage her MS.  Patient had tolerated Copaxone well. She did miss some doses during the past year when her husband was ill. She complains of injection site swelling but otherwise had been stable on Copaxone.
