gold_text: * * *  HISTORICAL DOCUMENT CONVERTED FROM CERNER  * * *    11/25/2009      Stalzer, MD  Department of Pediatrics  Palo Alto Medical Foundation  71 Lbsc Aly, Lake Havasu City AZ       RE: Tadayon, Seibert  MEDICAL RECORD #: [000000]  DATE OF BIRTH: 08/27/1996    Dear Doctor Mcneely:    Luca Tadayon returned to our Health After Therapy (HAT) Clinic for patients who  have been treated for childhood cancer.  He was accompanied by his mother today and  was last seen in this clinic on 12/10/2008.    Identification:  As you are aware of Luca's past medical history, I will summarize  this for our records and today's visit.    Luca Tadayon is a 13-year-old young man with a history of unilateral sporadic  retinoblastoma diagnosed in 07/25/1999 at the age of 3 years old.  He presented  with a 3-week history of redness and irritation in both of his eyes, and he was  seen by Dr. Aakash Kube, an ophthalmologist in San Elizario , New York, where the  family was living at that time.  Doctor Keri's feeling was that the patient  had retinoblastoma in his right eye, and he was referred to Gallatin  in  Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.  He was seen by  Dr. Henry Rossini and in 07/25/1999,   he underwent an examination under anesthesia, which demonstrated a solitary  retinoblastoma tumor in the inferonasal quadrant of the patient's right eye.  Signs  of retinal seeding were also noted between 6 and 7 o'clock in the retinal  periphery.  An examination of the macula showed prominent solitary mature seeding,  overlying the macular region.  Based on the patient's age at diagnosis as well  as examination of the contralateral eye, which was normal, the patient was  diagnosed with a sporadic unilateral retinoblastoma of the right eye.    Initial discussion of therapy included the following options:  Enucleation,  external beam radiotherapy, Gladstone application and chemotherapy  reduction.  At  that time, it was the decision of Dr. Press and Scheel's family to proceed with  chemotherapy reduction.  According to the mother's memory, he received chemotherapy  beginning in late 06/24/1999 to 12/25/1999.  It consisted of 6 cycles of  chemotherapy with the following agents:  Vincristine, etoposide and carboplatin.  This was also followed by cryotherapy to the areas of the seeding.  Serial  examinations under anesthesia revealed retinoblastoma of the infranasal quadrant of  the right eye with a lesion measuring 8 x 7 x 4 mm.  Vitreous seeding was observed  following chemotherapy reduction, to be extending from the tumor to the optic  nerve, and the patient received cryotherapy through several applications.  Unfortunately, the patient's vitreous seeding did not respond well to the  chemotherapy, with increased growth, which was noted on examination under  anesthesia in 03/25/2000.    The family then sought further opinions from other ocular oncologists, including  UCSF, and Dr. Mouhamed Rocker at UCLA.  It was the option of these consultants that  Luca would be better to undergo enucleation rather than radiotherapy.    Ultimately, the family decided to allow Schooler to undergo enucleation, which  occurred on 03/24/2000.  Following his enucleation, he was followed by  examinations under anesthesia at 6-month to 1-year intervals until the age of 5.    The family moved to Croton-on-Hudson NY  approximately 6-7 years ago and continued  follow-up care with Dr. Karl Vine, an ocular oncologist at Stanford  University.  Currently, Luca is followed by Doctor Lucia, an ophthalmologist at  Palo Alto Medical Foundation in Buchanan , California.  He has continued to do well  without any evidence of tumor recurrence and has his prosthesis refitted annually.      Internal History/Review of Systems:  According to Spies's mother and Luca, he  began to experience sharp pain on the left side of his chest  approximately 2 weeks  ago.  Luca describes these sharp pains as intense and almost "feeling like I was  having a heart attack."  He was seen by his primary care physician, Doctor Turlington,  and an EKG was performed to rule out any heart abnormalities.  At that time, it was  found that Nebel had first degree heart block on his echocardiogram.  He was   prescribed Prilosec for heartburn and, according to Saperstein, the pain as well as  the frequency has significantly decreased.    Luca's mother has relayed her concern over this first degree heart block, but  Luca has had no other physical symptoms, such as shortness of breath, unexplained  coughing, orthopnea, changes in his endurance or any other cardiac signs.    According to the history, he has had no unexplained fevers, illnesses, infections,  weight loss, pain, headaches, dizziness, ringing in his ears or buzzing sounds.    In the past, Luca has broken a toe, which he remembers hitting against a stair,  and he had a broken thumb, which was splinted.    Elimination Pattern:  There has been no history of blood in his stools,  constipation, diarrhea, hematuria, dysuria or frequency.    Sleep Pattern:  He has no difficulty getting to sleep or staying asleep, and  normally he receives 8-9 hours of sleep per night.    Vaccinations:  Up-to-date by history, and he has received the influenza vaccination  for 2009.    Dental Care:  Seen by his dentist, yesterday.    Vision Examination:  One month ago.    MEDICATIONS:  1.  Prilosec in the morning.  2.  He has a foot ointment used for his psoriasis.    ALLERGIES:  NO KNOWN ALLERGIES TO MEDICATIONS OR FOODS.    Diet History:  For breakfast, Troutman will have eggs, orange juice or cold cereal,  along with milk.  For lunch, it is usually pasta, salad, pizza or other varieties  of foods, and he will have a soda occasionally and/or milk.  For snacks, these  usually include fresh fruit, crackers and cheese.  For dinner, it is  chicken,  another form of protein, broccoli, salad and a carbohydrate.  He will drink  approximately 2-3 glasses of milk per day. For sodas, it is usually 1 a week, and  he choses to drink Diet Coke.  Denies drinking other caffeine drinks, such as coffee,  tea or energy drinks.    Social History:  Zeringue is an 8th grads student at Pinewood School, and he receives  A's and B's.  His favorite class is social studies, and the least favorite is  writing.    Exercise:  Mckenna participates in exercise frequently during the week, which  includes basketball, biking for fun, and he will be on the tennis team in  the spring.    Social Habits:  With his mother out of the room, I questioned Venugopal regarding his  friends as well as himself regarding tobacco, marijuana, alcohol or any other drug  use.  He denied trying any of these and also has no friends who have tried tobacco,  marijuana, alcohol or any other recreational drugs.    He resides with his 15-year-old sister along with both parents and a cat in Morton , California.    Family History:  His mother states that she is healthy, along with her husband.    The paternal grandfather had Fontenot's lymphoma and is deceased at the age of  81.  His paternal grandmother has diabetes, breast cancer twice, along with a  thyroid cancer, and she has heart problems.  She is currently 80 years of age.      The maternal grandfather is deceased from stomach cancer at the age of 83, and the  maternal grandmother is decreased at age 86 (in 2008) from lung cancer, and was  a known smoker.    Physical Examination:  Vital Signs:  His temperature is 36.8, pulse 81, respiratory rate 16, blood  pressure 104/67, weight 40.2 kg, height 159.5 cm.  General:  He is an engaging but slightly quiet adolescent male in no acute  distress.  Skin:  Clear with a few nevi.  HEENT:  Head is normocephalic with normal hair distribution.  Eyes:  The left eye  red reflex is seen, funduscopic exam, pupil is  equal and reactive to light,  conjunctiva clear.  His right has a prosthesis.  Nose is patent.  Oropharynx is  clear.  Teeth with good dentition.  Neck:  Supple.  Lymph:  No adenopathy.  Cardiovascular:  Regular rate and rhythm without murmur.  Chest:  Clear without any wheezing, rales or rhonchi.  Abdomen:  Soft and nontender, no hepatosplenomegaly.  GU:  Normal Jose stage III.  Extremities:  Full range of motion with no swelling or bony tenderness.  Neurologic:  Grossly intact.    Laboratory Data:  A CBC performed on 11/28/2009 at the Palo Alto Medical  Foundation shows a white count of 6.5, hemoglobin 13.8, hematocrit 40.3, platelet  count 292, MCV 82, MCHC 28.1.  RDW 12.7. Differential:  Neutrophils 51,  lymphocytes, 38, monocytes 8, eosinophils 2, basophils 1.    A chemistry panel, performed on 11/28/2009, showed a sodium of 139,  potassium 4.1, chloride 100, CO2 27, glucose 114, BUN 16, creatinine 0.6.  Calcium,  total protein, albumin, AST, ALT and alkaline phosphatase were normal.    Assessment:  Veer Klosterman is a 13-year-old young man who was diagnosed with  unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma in 07/25/1999 while living in New York.  He  received chemotherapy with 6 cycles, consisting of vincristine, etoposide and  carboplatin, which he completed in 12/25/1999.  It was found that he had  vitreous seeding following the chemotherapy.  He underwent enucleation of his right  eye on 03/24/2000 and has been followed closely by examinations under  anesthesia, and he shows no evidence of recurrent tumor.  It has now been 9 years  11 months since he completed his chemotherapy, and 9 years and 8 months since the  enucleation of his right eye.    It was discovered by surveillance EKG, since Luca was complaining of heartburn,  that he has a first degree heart block.  It appears that Luca has no physical  limitations, and there are no other cardiac signs or symptoms.    Plan:  1.  I spoke  with Quick's mother regarding his first degree heart block and also  provided her with the American Heart Association article regarding first degree  heart block, and there should be no concerns for his cardiac health at this  time.    2.  He will be referred to Cancer Genetics at Meeteetse  since  Luca's grandparents on both the maternal and paternal sides of the family have  significant history for cancer in all 4 grandparents.    3.  Articles titled "Health Link" provided by the Children's Oncology Group were  given to Scerbo and his mother.    The first article is regarding dental health since Zeigler received chemotherapy  at a young age.  There can be late effects from chemotherapy, such as absent  roots, poor enamel coloring, such as pitting or white spots, and an increased  number of caries.    An article on skin health was given since the Health After Therapy Clinic  emphasizes maintaining wellness, and we suggest using sunscreen with an SPF of 50  or greater along with sun-protective clothing.  Luca is an active  young man, and  he participates in tennis, bicycle riding and other outdoor recreational  activities.    A Health Link article addressing secondary cancers was given, since Lunt   received 6 cycles of etoposide, and we are unsure of the cumulative dose he   received at the age of 3.  This is mainly for information regarding this late  effect, and we will be checking his CBC on an annual basis for any abnormalities.    An article was given on hearing loss after being treated, since  he received carboplatin.  According to his mother, he has had a recent  evaluation by audiology with no hearing loss.    If any further information is needed regarding this clinic visit on Luca  Tadayon, please do not hesitate to contact us at 999-999-9999.  Oshea will be  returning to our Health After Clinic in 1 year.    Sincerely,      {END}        DOC #:  [000000]     DICT JOB #:   [000000]  Electronically signed on 12/01/2009    __________________________________________________________  Verna  Mitchell          Electronically signed on 12/01/2009    ___________________________________________________________  Lumsden MD, Hematology/Oncology

Ultimately, the family decided to allow Schooler to undergo enucleation, which  occurred on 03/24/2000.  Following his enucleation, he was followed by  examinations under anesthesia at 6-month to 1-year intervals until the age of 5.    The family moved to Croton-on-Hudson NY  approximately 6-7 years ago and continued  follow-up care with Dr. Karl Vine, an ocular oncologist at Stanford  University.  Currently, Luca is followed by Doctor Lucia, an ophthalmologist at  Palo Alto Medical Foundation in Buchanan , California.  He has continued to do well  without any evidence of tumor recurrence and has his prosthesis refitted annually.      Internal History/Review of Systems:  According to Spies's mother and Luca, he  began to experience sharp pain on the left side of his chest  approximately 2 weeks  ago.  Luca describes these sharp pains as intense and almost "feeling like I was  having a heart attack."  He was seen by his primary care physician, Doctor Turlington,  and an EKG was performed to rule out any heart abnormalities.  At that time, it was  found that Nebel had first degree heart block on his echocardiogram.  He was   prescribed Prilosec for heartburn and, according to Saperstein, the pain as well as  the frequency has significantly decreased.    Luca's mother has relayed her concern over this first degree heart block, but  Luca has had no other physical symptoms, such as shortness of breath, unexplained  coughing, orthopnea, changes in his endurance or any other cardiac signs.    According to the history, he has had no unexplained fevers, illnesses, infections,  weight loss, pain, headaches, dizziness, ringing in his ears or buzzing sounds.    In the past, Luca has broken a toe, which he remembers hitting against a stair,  and he had a broken thumb, which was splinted.    Elimination Pattern:  There has been no history of blood in his stools,  constipation, diarrhea, hematuria, dysuria or frequency.    Sleep Pattern:  He has no difficulty getting to sleep or staying asleep, and  normally he receives 8-9 hours of sleep per night.    Vaccinations:  Up-to-date by history, and he has received the influenza vaccination  for 2009.    Dental Care:  Seen by his dentist, yesterday.    Vision Examination:  One month ago.    MEDICATIONS:  1.  Prilosec in the morning.  2.  He has a foot ointment used for his psoriasis.    ALLERGIES:  NO KNOWN ALLERGIES TO MEDICATIONS OR FOODS.    Diet History:  For breakfast, Troutman will have eggs, orange juice or cold cereal,  along with milk.  For lunch, it is usually pasta, salad, pizza or other varieties  of foods, and he will have a soda occasionally and/or milk.  For snacks, these  usually include fresh fruit, crackers and cheese.  For dinner, it is  chicken,  another form of protein, broccoli, salad and a carbohydrate.  He will drink  approximately 2-3 glasses of milk per day. For sodas, it is usually 1 a week, and  he choses to drink Diet Coke.  Denies drinking other caffeine drinks, such as coffee,  tea or energy drinks.    Social History:  Zeringue is an 8th grads student at Pinewood School, and he receives  A's and B's.  His favorite class is social studies, and the least favorite is  writing.    Exercise:  Mckenna participates in exercise frequently during the week, which  includes basketball, biking for fun, and he will be on the tennis team in  the spring.    Social Habits:  With his mother out of the room, I questioned Venugopal regarding his  friends as well as himself regarding tobacco, marijuana, alcohol or any other drug  use.  He denied trying any of these and also has no friends who have tried tobacco,  marijuana, alcohol or any other recreational drugs.    He resides with his 15-year-old sister along with both parents and a cat in Morton , California.    Family History:  His mother states that she is healthy, along with her husband.    The paternal grandfather had Fontenot's lymphoma and is deceased at the age of  81.  His paternal grandmother has diabetes, breast cancer twice, along with a  thyroid cancer, and she has heart problems.  She is currently 80 years of age.      The maternal grandfather is deceased from stomach cancer at the age of 83, and the  maternal grandmother is decreased at age 86 (in 2008) from lung cancer, and was  a known smoker.    Physical Examination:  Vital Signs:  His temperature is 36.8, pulse 81, respiratory rate 16, blood  pressure 104/67, weight 40.2 kg, height 159.5 cm.  General:  He is an engaging but slightly quiet adolescent male in no acute  distress.  Skin:  Clear with a few nevi.  HEENT:  Head is normocephalic with normal hair distribution.  Eyes:  The left eye  red reflex is seen, funduscopic exam, pupil is  equal and reactive to light,  conjunctiva clear.  His right has a prosthesis.  Nose is patent.  Oropharynx is  clear.  Teeth with good dentition.  Neck:  Supple.  Lymph:  No adenopathy.  Cardiovascular:  Regular rate and rhythm without murmur.  Chest:  Clear without any wheezing, rales or rhonchi.  Abdomen:  Soft and nontender, no hepatosplenomegaly.  GU:  Normal Jose stage III.  Extremities:  Full range of motion with no swelling or bony tenderness.  Neurologic:  Grossly intact.    Laboratory Data:  A CBC performed on 11/28/2009 at the Palo Alto Medical  Foundation shows a white count of 6.5, hemoglobin 13.8, hematocrit 40.3, platelet  count 292, MCV 82, MCHC 28.1.  RDW 12.7. Differential:  Neutrophils 51,  lymphocytes, 38, monocytes 8, eosinophils 2, basophils 1.    A chemistry panel, performed on 11/28/2009, showed a sodium of 139,  potassium 4.1, chloride 100, CO2 27, glucose 114, BUN 16, creatinine 0.6.  Calcium,  total protein, albumin, AST, ALT and alkaline phosphatase were normal.    Assessment:  Veer Klosterman is a 13-year-old young man who was diagnosed with  unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma in 07/25/1999 while living in New York.  He  received chemotherapy with 6 cycles, consisting of vincristine, etoposide and  carboplatin, which he completed in 12/25/1999.  It was found that he had  vitreous seeding following the chemotherapy.  He underwent enucleation of his right  eye on 03/24/2000 and has been followed closely by examinations under  anesthesia, and he shows no evidence of recurrent tumor.  It has now been 9 years  11 months since he completed his chemotherapy, and 9 years and 8 months since the  enucleation of his right eye.    It was discovered by surveillance EKG, since Luca was complaining of heartburn,  that he has a first degree heart block.  It appears that Luca has no physical  limitations, and there are no other cardiac signs or symptoms.    Plan:  1.  I spoke  with Quick's mother regarding his first degree heart block and also  provided her with the American Heart Association article regarding first degree  heart block, and there should be no concerns for his cardiac health at this  time.    2.  He will be referred to Cancer Genetics at Meeteetse  since  Luca's grandparents on both the maternal and paternal sides of the family have  significant history for cancer in all 4 grandparents.    3.  Articles titled "Health Link" provided by the Children's Oncology Group were  given to Scerbo and his mother.    The first article is regarding dental health since Zeigler received chemotherapy  at a young age.  There can be late effects from chemotherapy, such as absent  roots, poor enamel coloring, such as pitting or white spots, and an increased  number of caries.    An article on skin health was given since the Health After Therapy Clinic  emphasizes maintaining wellness, and we suggest using sunscreen with an SPF of 50  or greater along with sun-protective clothing.  Luca is an active  young man, and  he participates in tennis, bicycle riding and other outdoor recreational  activities.    A Health Link article addressing secondary cancers was given, since Lunt   received 6 cycles of etoposide, and we are unsure of the cumulative dose he   received at the age of 3.  This is mainly for information regarding this late  effect, and we will be checking his CBC on an annual basis for any abnormalities.    An article was given on hearing loss after being treated, since  he received carboplatin.  According to his mother, he has had a recent  evaluation by audiology with no hearing loss.    If any further information is needed regarding this clinic visit on Luca  Tadayon, please do not hesitate to contact us at 999-999-9999.  Oshea will be  returning to our Health After Clinic in 1 year.    Sincerely,      {END}        DOC #:  [000000]     DICT JOB #:   [000000]  Electronically signed on 12/01/2009    __________________________________________________________  Verna  Mitchell          Electronically signed on 12/01/2009    ___________________________________________________________  Lumsden MD, Hematology/Oncology

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED:
FRONTAL SCALP SKIN BIOPSY, ALOPECIA

SUBMITTED ICD9 CODE:  L65.9


DIAGNOSIS:
SKIN, FRONTAL SCALP SKIN BIOPSY, ALOPECIA, BIOPSY
           --          PAUCI-INFLAMMATORY, NON-SCARRING ALOPECIA WITH MILD MINIATURIZATION AND MILD CATAGEN/TELOGEN SHIFT (SEE COMMENT)
Belle Fourche /SALEEM/Freiman

COMMENT: The predominant findings include a mild catagen/telogen shift and mildly increased miniaturization. We favor a diagnosis of androgenetic alopecia, with the catagen/telogen shift suggesting superimposed telogen effluvium. No peribulbar inflammation is seen to support alopecia areata. There is no evidence of a scarring alopecia. Clinical correlation is recommended.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:  Horizontal sections demonstrate 40 hair follicles per transverse section at the level of the mid dermis.  Mild miniaturization of follicles is identified (15/40), and there is also a mild catagen-telogen shift (12/40). At the level of the isthmus, sebaceous lobules are preserved. No perifollicular fibrosis is identified. There is scant lymphocytic inflammation in the stroma with neither interface activity nor investment of the follicular epithelium.  No peribulbar inflammation is present. PASd staining is negative for fungal organisms and fails to highlight basement membrane thickening or disruption.

CLINICAL HISTORY: 36-year-old female with positive tug test, some miniaturization of the hair follicles, and slowly progressive loss of hair density over the past 1 year

CLINICAL IMPRESSION: Androgenetic alopecia versus telogen effluvium

GROSS DESCRIPTION: Specimen is received labeled with the patient's name and medical record number.
The specimen labeled "frontal scalp" is received in formalin and consists of a 0.4 x 0.4 cm unoriented skin punch biopsy with an excised depth of 0.2 cm. The resection margin is inked blue, and entirely submitted in cassette A1.   Fernando Faillace (03/10/2022)





I have reviewed the specimen and agree with the interpretation above. Ayana Fuqua M.D., Ph.D.   Electronically signed 03/13/2022

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Mr. Amos is a 31-year-old white  gentleman with a history of depression and hypertension who presented to  the emergency room on the evening of 03/01/2024 complaining of left  retro-orbital pain associated with slurred speech and inappropriate words.  The patient stated that his symptoms began around 10 a.m., shortly after  awakening, with problems with slurred speech and inappropriate words. The  patient said he had some numbness and tingling in his left hand and, most  remarkably, the tips of his fingers. The patient denied any pain or other  numbness at that time. The patient states that he has some baseline  left-sided weakness, which is mild, but that his weakness did not become  acutely worse during this episode. The patient said that symptoms  continued until he developed acute onset of left-sided retro-orbital pain  that began around 6 p.m. the evening of admission. The pain was rated at  approximately 7/10. The patient denied any other symptoms, such as  palpitations, shortness of breath, or nausea. The patient stated that he  had some blurry vision, but no flashing lights; the patient denied any  diplopia. The patient said that he also developed some fatigue at this  time.    The patient states that this type of thing has happened to him before. He  has had three episodes, the first of which was in 10/27/2001. The  patient states that he began noticing that he felt a little bit lightheaded  and had some problems with balance. The patient stated that an hour later,  he noticed that his left hand was remarkably weak and numb. The patient  stated that he had some numbness of his lips and blurry vision. The  patient also developed fairly acute onset left-sided retro-orbital pain  that he rated at approximately  9/10. The patient also recalled having  problems with word finding and speaking inappropriate words at that time.  He stated that he also had two additional episodes between 10/27/2001 and  the current presentation. During those episodes, the patient stated that  his symptoms and his headaches were significantly less severe. The patient  rated those headaches at approximately 4/10 to 5/10. In all of these  episodes, the patient denied any palpitations, shortness of breath, nausea,  or diplopia. The patient states that he had blurry vision and some fatigue  associated with these episodes.    During his first episode, the patient presented to an emergency room in  Delta , where he was told that he had a complicated migraine. The patient  was discharged from the ER with a referral to Dr. Zechariah Zambito, a  neurologist. The patient saw Dr. Padgett after his first episode, and he  had an MRI. Based on that MRI, the patient states that Dr. Ghosh told  him that he may have had a mild stroke. The patient presents to the  emergency room today because he was concerned that he may have had another  stroke.    PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Depression, hypertension over the past five years;  the patient says that he has hypertensive episodes with blood pressure  going up to 160/120 over a baseline blood pressure of 120/70 on medication.  The patient states that he feels "strange" when he has these hypertensive  episodes.    MEDICATIONS  1. Diovan 160 mg q.d.  2. Propanolol 20 mg b.i.d.  3. Effexor XR 150 mg q.d.    SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient is an undergraduate student studying English at  Alonie College. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. The patient lives at home  with his partner.    FAMILY HISTORY: The patient does not know his father. The patient states  that his mother is 69 years old and has hypertension, a thyroid condition,  and some heart problems. The patient has a brother who had a seizure as a  young child, but does not  have epilepsy.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: The patient denied any chest pain, shortness of breath,  nausea or vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, or hematuria. The  patient denies any heat or cold intolerance. The patient denies any head  trauma, any history of intracranial infections, or any family history of  seizures.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: VITAL SIGNS: Stable and unremarkable. NEUROLOGICAL:  The patient was alert and oriented x 3. He was grossly cognitively intact.  The patient had fluid speech, with normal naming and repetition. There was  no obvious aphasia or dysarthria at the time of the exam. His pupils were  equally round and reactive to light. Extraocular movements were intact.  There was no nystagmus. His tongue and palate were midline. Facial  expression was symmetric. Facial sensation was intact and symmetric as  well. The patient's hearing was intact. The patient's sternocleidomastoid  and trapezius were 5/5 in strength. The patient's motor strength was 5/5  in all extremities. He had normal tone and bulk. There was no pronator  drift. The patient's finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin were within normal  limits. His sensation was intact to light touch and symmetric. His gait  was unremarkable. The patient had a normal Romberg. His reflexes were 2+  throughout, and his toes were downgoing bilaterally.    LABORATORY STUDIES: The patient had an unremarkable chem-7. He had a CBC  that was only remarkable for mildly elevated white count at 12.6.    IMAGING STUDIES: The patient's head CT demonstrated no acute disease.  There was no bleed and no evidence of an old infarct. Review of the  patient's MRI, which the patient brought with him to the emergency room,  demonstrated a 1 x 4-mm area that was bright on FLAIR. This abnormality  was seen only on one slice and was located superior to the left basal  ganglia. This abnormality was also subtly appreciated on T2 imaging. This  abnormality was not seen on T1  imaging and did not enhance on the post  Gadolinium images. The  patient said he had some numbness and tingling in his left hand and, most  remarkably, the tips of his fingers. The patient denied any pain or other  numbness at that time. The patient states that he has some baseline  left-sided weakness, which is mild, but that his weakness did not become  acutely worse during this episode. The patient said that symptoms  continued until he developed acute onset of left-sided retro-orbital pain  that began around 6 p.m. the evening of admission. The pain was rated at  approximately 7/10. The patient denied any other symptoms, such as  palpitations, shortness of breath, or nausea. The patient stated that he  had some blurry vision, but no flashing lights; the patient denied any  diplopia. The patient said that he also developed some fatigue at this  time.    The patient states that this type of thing has happened to him before. He  has had three episodes, the first of which was in 10/27/2001. The  patient states that he began noticing that he felt a little bit lightheaded  and had some problems with balance. The patient stated that an hour later,  he noticed that his left hand was remarkably weak and numb. The patient  stated that he had some numbness of his lips and blurry vision. She is accompanied by her mother, from whom additional history was obtained.    History of Present Illness:  i  In 12/24/2018, Nyesha had a mild TBI from a sports injury (hit in the head with a water polo ball). Afterwards, she had dizziness, headache, and difficulty concentrating. Her symptoms resolved over 5-6 weeks.    Then in 01/24/2019, she was hit again in the head (similar mechanism). She again had dizziness, headache, and difficulty concentrating that resolved over 5-6 weeks.    Starting 07/24/2019, she developed new daily headache. Her headache has continued constantly and unremitting since onset.    In 03/24/2020, she obtained a brain MRI that was reportedly normal.    In 12/24/2019, started seeing a therapist for anxiety and depression.    No prior history of headache.  No family history of headache or migraine.    Headaches:  #1: Constant headache  Description of pain: dull  Localization: bitemporal/frontal  Severity: 3-4/10  Duration: constant, unremitting  Frequency: daily  Associated symptoms: tinnitus (worsens with sitting to standing) without vertigo/nausea. No photophobia/phonophobia.  Alleviated with ice packs. Tried Advil/Tylenol (no effect).    #2: "Migraines"  Description of pain: dull, pressure  Localization: bifrontal to diffuse. Radiates down her neck.  Severity:  6/10  Duration: 1-3 hours  Frequency: 3-4x/wk. Random, all times of day. No trigger.  Associated symptoms: No vision change. No nausea/vomiting. No photophobia/phonophobia.  Alleviated with rest and ice pack.    #3: Fire pain  Description of pain:dull, burning  Localization: bifrontal  Severity: 5/10  Duration: 2-3 hrs  Frequency: 2x/wk  Associated symptoms: stress/concentration. No nausea/vomiting. No photophobia/phonophobia.  Improves with stopping studying/screen time.    No ptosis, miosis, lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea, and nasal congestion    No pain with laying down, nighttime awakening, pain worst in the morning, urinary/bowel incontinence, weakness, abnormal gait, sensory changes.    No associated with menses    Missing a significant amount of school. Grades decreased from all As to a few Bs.    Sleep: 10-11 hours of sleep. No nighttime awakening. No snoring.  Drinking: 1-2 liter of water per day  Eating: No skipped meals  Mood: Anxious  Caffeine: None  Physical activity: Walks, core/shoulder workouts      Review of Systems:   i  A complete 14-point review of systems is negative, except as noted in the HPI.     Medications:    No current outpatient medications on file.       Allergies:   Patient has no known allergies.    Past History:  i  Birth History:  - Former FT born via cesarean (shoulder dystocia) with no complications during pregnancy or delivery  - vigorous on delivery  - home with parents  - mild familial neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, no phototherapy needed    Past Medical History: None  Past Surgical History: None  Development History:   - no issues form parents or PCP regarding early developmetal milestones  -- sit independently at 6 mos  - walked at 9 mos  - first words at 12 mos  - no learning concerns, no IEP    Family History:  There is otherwise no family history of developmental delay, learning problems, seizures, brain tumors, or other neurologic disorders.     Social  History:    -- lives at home with mother, father, brother  -- starting 12th grade in the Fall. A/Bs.    Physical Examination:  i  Vital Signs:  There were no vitals taken for this visit.  GENERAL:  Gen: well-appearing, well-devleoped, no acute distress, anxious  HEENT: Normocephalic, Sclera and conjunctiva clear  Pulm: Comfortable work of breathing    NEUROLOGIC EXAM:  Mental Status: Awake, alert, speaks in complete sentences with vocabulary appropriate for age and with fluent language. Follows instructions well.  Cranial Nerves: Pupils equal and round.  EOMI with conjugate gaze.  No ptosis or nystagmus.  Symmetrical facial movements. Tongue protrudes midline. Symmetric elevation of palate.  No dysarthria. Symmetric shoulder shrug.  Motor: Normal muscle bulk throughout.  No pronator drift.  No extraneous movements. Intact and symmetric rapid finger taps bilaterally. Intact alternating hand movements bilaterally. No obvious focal deficits of strength.  Sensory:  Deferred  Deep Tendon Reflexes: Deferred  Coordination: FNF intact without dysmetria.  No truncal titubation.  Gait: Normal station and stride.  Walks on heels and toes with ease.  Tandem gait is intact.     I have reviewed the following reports and have reviewed the imaging studies, and agree with the findings outline below.   Labs:  None  Imaging:  None  EEG:  None    Review of Outside Records:  None    ASSESSMENT:  Nyesha is a 16-year old female with a history of multiple prior head injuries with new development of a chronic daily headache.    Her exam was normal and the reportedly normal brain MRI is even further reassuring against secondary etiology for her headache.    She most likely has a primary headache disorder. Given the duration of her headache, she has a chronic daily headache with migrainous features (tinnitus). Her other headaches are likely exacerbation of her daily headache. Chronic tension-type headache is possible given the bilateral,  nonthrobbing "featureless" characterization with moderate intensity. Based on description, it is unlikely that she has acute on chronic migraines.    She has had a year of chronic daily headache and will hopefully benefit from pharmacologic preventative medication. While we do not expect her headache to entirely remit, our goal will be improvement in any feature of her headaches. We discussed the necessity of a headache diary to track gradual changes. We discussed lifestyle modifications, many of which she is already at goal.    The encounter diagnosis was Chronic daily headache.    Patient Active Problem List    Diagnosis Date Noted   • Chronic daily headache 08/01/2020     PLAN:   1. Keep headache diary until next visit  2. Encouraged lifestyle modifications: hydration, regular meals (no skipping meals), aerobic exercise (30 minutes at least three times a week), sleep regulation and standardized sleep/wake schedule, limit caffeine intake  3. Start daily nutraceutical prophylaxis:  i. Magnesium oxide- 400 mg/day for at least 3 months (main side effect is diarrhea or loose stool, can titrate to effect)  ii. Riboflavin (vitamin B2)- 400 mg daily for at least 3 months (may cause urine discoloration to orange or red hue)  4. Start Topiramate 25mg daily  5. F/u in 3 months via telehealth    QI: Seizures Precautions and First-Aid Discussed with Patient/Family? N/A     I discussed the plan and all recommendations with the family and patient and they expressed understanding and are in agreement of the plan.     Thank you for allowing me to participate in the care of your patient. Please do not hesitate to contact me for any questions or concerns at 999-999-9999.     Note completed by: Leandro Weddle, MD  Teaching Physician Attestation     I saw the patient and discussed her management with the resident. I reviewed the resident's note and agree with the documented findings and plan of care. A total of 15 minutes was  spent face to face with the family discussing headaches, treatments and need for further work up.   Skalski, MD

gold_text: Well Child Notes - 3 Years      Nutrition:     There is a wide range of what is considered a normal eating pattern at this age.  It is all right if your child seems to eat very little on some days.  Children this age often balance their diets over a week or more, rather than during a single day.  Your job is to offer a variety of healthy foods.  Your child’s job is to decide how much his body needs that day.    Eating together as a family is important.  Sitting and talking at mealtime establishes good bonds between family members.  At this age, children usually have three meals and one to two snacks a day.  Just like meals, snacks should be mostly healthy foods.  In general, try to stay away from sugary foods like crackers, cookies or puffs.  Give your child many chances to try a new food.  Continue to offer foods that your child has rejected.  It’s OK to hide vegetables in things like pasta sauce, but continue to offer vegetables undisguised, too.  Over time, his tastes will likely change and he may become more interested in a wider variety of foods - maybe even vegetables!                We recommend 16 to 24 ounces of unflavored milk each day, which provides good amounts of calcium and vitamin D.   If your child doesn’t drink milk, there are also other ways to get calcium and vitamin D.  Let us know if you’d like our handouts about these nutrients.  Water, not juice, is the best additional drink to offer throughout the day.  Juice does not offer much in the way of nutrition, so if you do offer juice, it should be limited to 4 ounces or less per day.        Dental:     Dentists advise brushing twice a day with just a little bit of fluoride-containing toothpaste.  Make sure to brush before bedtime and then don’t let your child eat or drink anything except water after his teeth are brushed.  Let your child brush first, but follow that up with your own brushing of his teeth.  If your child has teeth that are close  enough to each other that they touch, you should also floss his teeth before bedtime.  If you haven’t already taken your child to the dentist, now is a good time to start - and continue with visits every six months, or as your dentist advises.    Development:      As this year progresses, you will see your child grow in many ways.  Usually tantrums are less frequent, and you can enjoy your child’s blossoming personality more and more.  However, she may frequently test you with bursts of negativity and opposition.  Fears and nightmares are common at this age.  “Why?” is a common question that three year olds ask.  Children appreciate being responsible for small things like setting the table or helping you cook.  Children this age become curious about their own body, including private parts - and “playing doctor” is normal and not worrisome behavior at this age.    By the end of this year, your child should be able to use sentences with more than three words, tell a coherent story, and speak clearly enough to be understood most of the time by strangers.  At this point she can tell you about things that happened during her day.  This is a good opportunity to talk about her feelings, friends, thoughts, and experiences.  Use everyday moments as opportunities to teach - whether it is about feelings, numbers, colors, or anything that presents itself.  She will probably be delighted if you encourage telling imaginary stories and engaging in fantasy play.  It is fun to sing songs,  play rhyming games and talk about pictures in books with your child - and it helps with her brain development as well!  She will likely begin to enjoy structured games like tag or catch.   She may also be able to learn to ride a tricycle.  If she does learn to ride a tricycle, please be sure she always wears a helmet when riding.  By the end of this year, she should be able to go up and down stairs without support and be able to hop on one foot.   She will likely begin to draw circles and even simple pictures.      Play and Toys:   This is an important year for the development of cooperative play.  Group play and activities with other children will help your child’s social and cognitive development.   Encourage her to interact with other children and help her understand the importance of taking turns.  Encourage outdoor activities and exercise, and also to play quietly on her own sometimes.  Good activities for personal play include building blocks, picture books, jigsaw puzzles (with four or five large pieces) and crayons.      Screen Time:    A child's brain develops rapidly during the first few years, and young children learn best by interacting with people, not screens.  They need to spend time playing outside, reading, playing with other people, and using their imaginations in free play.  For optimal development, it is best to limit combined screen time to no more than 1-2 hours a day in total.  Screen time includes computer, smart phone, or tablet time as well as time in front of the TV.  Make sure the content is age appropriate.  Also, it helps if you watch together and discuss what you both see and think.  It is especially important to avoid watching TV during family time and before bed.  Even TV that is just on in the background may impact child development negatively.  Also, it is wise to avoid scary and/or violent TV shows, movies and video games.      Sleep Hygiene:  Most three year olds function best with at least 10-12 hours of sleep at night.  Try to have a good, structured bedtime routine.  Do not let your child stall.  Calm activities prior to bed are better than vigorous or involved activities.  Some children at this age still take a nap but many children will grow out of taking a nap by the end of this year.    Discipline:  The word discipline comes from the root “to teach.”  Model respectful behavior by being respectful to your child and  to other people.  Children tend to imitate their parents.  Ask your child about his day, listen to the answers and let him finish his sentences - even if it takes forever!  Discuss feelings as they happen.  This will help develop “emotional intelligence.”  Emotional intelligence is the ability to perceive and evaluate one’s own emotions.  This is a very important skill to have.  Finally, giving loving attention helps to develop the bond that makes your child want to behave well.  We recommend giving praise and positive attention for behaviors you want to encourage and using “timeouts” for behaviors that you want your child to stop, such as hitting, kicking or treating others poorly.  Praise works best when it is for concrete actions.  For example, “I noticed that you were kind to the new child in the group and shared your toy with her,” or “I appreciated the smile and positive attitude this morning.”  If your child is engaging in destructive behavior, sometimes just noticing the consequences is enough - “you yelled too loudly so we had to leave the library.”  But sometimes a child needs a timeout if the behavior repeats itself.  You can give your child warnings, such as “three strikes.”  If he persists in the bad behavior, send him to a timeout area that is boring, like a chair or a corner of the room.  One minute per year of age is sufficient punishment (for example, 3 minutes of time out for a 3 year old).  Using a timer helps him know when the timeout will be over.  If your child leaves the timeout area, take him back and restart the timer.  Do not spank your child - several studies have shown that children who are spanked tend to have more problems with behavior in the future as well as problems with psychological development.    Toilet Training:    It is great to read books about toilet training with your child.  Take your child to the bathroom and praise her for sitting on the potty or toilet even with her  clothes on.  Plan on needing frequent toilet breaks - children use the toilet as many as 10 times each day.  Help your child wash her hands after using the toilet, after diaper changes and before meals.     Safety Suggestions:  • Use a properly installed forward-facing car seat in the back seat of your car.  At this age we recommend continuing to use a seat with a 5-point harness rather than a seat-belt-positioned booster seat, as long as your child still is under the manufacturer’s weight and height limits for your car seat.    • Supervise all play near streets, driveways and parking lots.  Always hold your child’s hand when crossing a street.  • Window guards are recommended on higher-story windows as well as safety gates at the top and bottom of staircases.  • Every six months check to be sure that your smoke and carbon monoxide detectors are working.  • Avoid too much sun exposure.  If you’ll be in the sun for a while, or in reflected light from water or snow, use hats, shades, clothing, and sunblock on exposed skin to protect your child from burning.    • Never leave your child unattended near water.  • Lock away cleaning supplies, medicines and guns.  • Put the Poison Control Center’s number on all phones: 1-999-999-9999.    For Parents…  • Consider taking a CPR class.  • Avoid smoking (tobacco or otherwise) - to quit smoking, call 1-800-NO-BUTTS (1-999-999-9999).  • Call for help if you are ever hit or hurt by someone and if you and your child are not safe at home.  National Domestic violence hotline is 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) - they can guide you to a safe place.  • If you feel overwhelmed, sad, or about to harm your child, call your doctor or a friend for help, or call 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-999-999-9999)  • Let us know if you are having problems and cannot make ends meet.  There are resources in our community to help you.  ?You can also call "211" on any phone (or www.example.com) to find organizations to  help with housing, mental health, free or low cost food, and much more.    Medicine for fever or pain - Acetaminophen (Tylenol) or Ibuprofen (Advil):    Talk to us about when you should use one of these, and ask us for a copy of our dosing chart.  It is not necessary to treat fever alone, but if your child is very fussy, or in a lot of pain, acetaminophen or ibuprofen may help.  Sometimes they may have side-effects, including a possible increased risk of asthma in susceptible patients.      V.06/19/2014

He presents today with his parents, from whom additional history was obtained.     As you will recall, Saeed is a very pleasant 10-year old male with a history of chronic tics for 5 years that mom believes are related to vaccines. Eye movements were most noticeable after vaccines at 5 years of age. They also worsened again after repeated shots that year per mom. He was seen at Cook Children's Hospital Neurology in Texas, and switched to Dr. Noland (Madera Neurology) 03/31/2019 when they moved to California.     While in Texas he bumped in to a corner and had black/blue eye of the left. He presented to the ER and he then started to have a hand jerk movements/tics (3 years ago). This affected his eating and writing. This is now less frequent but returns when he is more stressed.      Tic History:  Onset: before kindergarten. Mom reports that less than 1 hour after getting immunizations for school his eye tic started.   5 yo: Started Guanfacine  2016-2019: Clonidine (mom reports that his memory issues and poor concentration are a side effect of this medication)  2019: 10 yo: Dr. Dave (Merced Neurology): Started Pimozide, took for 2 months but weaned off due to worsening memory issues (mom reports that he has always had memory issues). Memory improved after but did not resolved. Given that mom read that clonidine has caused memory issues as well so they did not restart this medication      Memory Issues: can not memorize his lines for an upcoming school play. After learning a math concept, he will forget it when approaching the next problem  Eating Disorder: diagnosed with parasites when  he was 4 that he was born with (thought it was from India). He took a medication to treat this. He has never wanted to eat (had to force feed him per mom). This did not improved after treatment of the parasite. After taking 2-3 bites of food he will say I am not hungry. UGI and colonoscopy were unremarkable.      Current Tics: Eye rolling movements that cause him to lose his place on a page while reading and causes frustration. He stretches his mouth in a smile or opens mouth wide. He is also teased by children at school due to his tics.       Medications Tried:  Alpha-2-agonist (guanfacine/clonidine): YES Guanfacine at 5 yo and switched to clonidine 0.1 mg daily due to lack of efficacy of guanfacine. He gets in trouble in class for talking to kids in class but parents report that concerns for ADD have not been raised at school. Stanford Sleep Health and Insomnia Program      Clinician: Abendroth, PhD     Referring Provider: Sum-Ping, Donell Orsak, MD     Individual Psychotherapy 55 min (CPT: 90837)     Diagnosis: Insomnia Disorder     Presenting Problem: Nylah Odalis Stark is a 32 Y old female who reports difficulty with sleep-related worry, early morning awakening, and difficulty falling asleep and staying asleep without sleep medications.     Follow Up Session # 4  I have discussed the risks, benefits, and limitations of receiving care virtually with the patient. The patient expresses understanding and is willing to move forward. Confirmed patient identity, location, and emergency contact information. Patient confirmed in private, secure space.      Summary statement:   The patient's sleep diaries were reviewed. Time in bed ranged from 11 pm to 11:45 pm. Sleep onset latency and wakefulness after sleep onset are within normal limits. Rise time ranged from 6:20 am to 7:05 am. Total sleep time ranged from 7-8 hours (estimated sleep efficiency = 94%). Patient was travelling and took trazodone and melatonin one nights out of the past 2 weeks.      In this session, the patient reported reported satisfaction with nighttime sleep quality. Collaboratively, we decided to expand her time in bed window by 15 minutes as her sleep efficiency remained high despite traveling across time zones. Patient endorsed high anticipatory anxiety about being unable to maintain treatment benefits long term. She also expressed worry about the fragility of her sleep. Provider used cognitive strategies (see below) to strengthen patient's sense of self-efficacy and remind her of the skills she has learned to manage her sleep in the context of various situations (e.g., travel, late night socializing, and during periods of stress). Time was spent troubleshooting adherence to previous recommendations. The patient¿s goals for treatment were reviewed and  progress towards goals discussed. Arousal reduction strategies aimed at ruminative thoughts, avoidance of daytime activities, and catastrophizing were discussed (see below).     Adherence: Patient maintained sleep diaries and demonstrated high adherence to treatment recommendaions     Progress in Treatment: Progress is high and continued work towards maintaining high sleep efficiency and reducing sleep-related anxiety is underway.     Recommendations and Plan:              1. Go to bed no earlier than 10:30 pm and maintain 6:45 am wake time. Rise no matter how you slept the night before.  2. Continue writing about your worries and stressful past experiences. Do this every day. Discuss common themes with your therapist and sit with the discomfort that comes up when you think about these things.   1. REMEMBER: Anxiety feeds off of avoidance. When we push things off, we might feel short term relief, but in the long run, our feelings of anxiety only increase.  2. The antidote for this is to do the things your anxiety is making you want to avoid. In small, tolerable chunks. This often increases our anxiety, initially, in the short term (we now have to do the thing we¿re afraid to do!), but if you practice doing this, your anxiety will decrease in the long-term. Eventually, you'll see how relieving it feels to do the things you¿ve been avoiding.  3. Continue wind down routine. This is a protected, valued time for you so do it even if it cuts into your sleep. Remember, you can always schedule a 20-30 minute nap or rest period the following day if needed. Just do it before 2 pm.  1. Even if you're feeling tired but not feeling sleepy, you can use this time to allow your body to rest, recover and recharge following a night of less sleep.  4. Continue wearing eye mask to help reduce early morning arousals caused by light.  5. Stay in bed and relax until your alarm goes off.  6. Continue to track sleep. Please message me your  sleep tracking before our next meeting.  7. Next visit scheduled for 12/27 at 2 pm with Dr. Augustin Asmus (999-999-9999.       Patient is receptive to CBT-I treatment.     Cinelli, Ph.D.  Postdoctoral Fellow  Stanford School of Medicine  Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences      Discussed patient with DR. Murr, reviewed session note and agree with treatment plan as documented.  Slotkin Huff, PhD

Patient was travelling and took trazodone and melatonin every night before bed, as recommended.     In this session, the patient reported reported high satisfaction with nighttime sleep quality and time in bed window. Patient is working towards maintaining sleep schedule in the context of a heavy travel schedule. We continued using cognitive strategies (see below) to strengthen patient's sense of self-efficacy and remind her of the skills she has learned to manage her sleep in the long-term. Time was spent troubleshooting adherence to previous recommendations. The patient¿s goals for treatment were reviewed and progress towards goals discussed. Arousal reduction strategies aimed at ruminative thoughts, avoidance of daytime activities, and catastrophizing were discussed (see  below).     Adherence: Patient maintained sleep diaries and demonstrated high adherence to treatment recommendaions     Progress in Treatment: Progress is high and continued work towards maintaining high sleep efficiency and reducing sleep-related anxiety is underway.     Stated Goal(s):   1. Reduce dependence on sleep medications (progress)  2. Live a fuller life and stop avoiding activities due to fear of insomnia (progress)    Recommendations and Plan:              1. Go to bed no earlier than 10:30 pm and maintain 6:45 am wake time. Rise no matter how you slept the night before.  2. Continue writing about your worries and stressful past experiences. Do this every day. Discuss common themes with your therapist and sit with the discomfort that comes up when you think about these things.   · REMEMBER: Anxiety feeds off of avoidance. When we push things off, we might feel short term relief, but in the long run, our feelings of anxiety only increase.  · The antidote for this is to do the things your anxiety is making you want to avoid. In small, tolerable chunks. This often increases our anxiety, initially, in the short term (we now have to do the thing we¿re afraid to do!), but if you practice doing this, your anxiety will decrease in the long-term. Eventually, you'll see how relieving it feels to do the things you¿ve been avoiding.  3. Continue wind down routine. This is a protected, valued time for you so do it even if it cuts into your sleep. Remember, you can always schedule a 20-30 minute nap or rest period the following day if needed. Just do it before 2 pm.  1. Even if you're feeling tired but not feeling sleepy, you can use this time to allow your body to rest, recover and recharge following a night of less sleep.  4. Continue wearing eye mask to help reduce early morning arousals caused by light.  5. Stay in bed and relax until your alarm goes off.  6. Continue to track sleep. Please message me your sleep  tracking before our next meeting.  7. Next visit scheduled for 1/11 at 2 pm with Dr. Lyon Oldham (999-999-9999.       Patient is receptive to CBT-I treatment.     Butcher, Ph.D.  Postdoctoral Fellow  Stanford School of Medicine  Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences      Discussed patient with Dr/ Zenda, reviewed session note and agree with treatment plan as documented.  Yusuf Johnson, PhD

gold_text: 02/04/2013    Aayan Victoria, MD  448 J40 Ln, Austin CO     Ollin Floyd, MD  794 Cnl Rd, Heyburn ID     PATIENT: Mr. Sophia Joffe Volk   MR NO: [000000]   DATE OF BIRTH: 08/23/1947   DATE OF VISIT: 02/04/2013     Dear Drs. Lacroix and Thakker,    I had the pleasure of seeing your patient, Mr. Sophia Joffe Volk, at the Stanford Center for Memory Disorders today in consultation for concern about memory function. He was accompanied to this visit by his longtime girlfriend Veksler, who participated in the history.    As you may be aware, Mr. Sophia Joffe Volk is a 65 Y right handed male with no significant medical history presenting with a chief complaint of " I may have Alzheimer's". He has been anxious about getting Alzheimer's disease for some time, because his father was diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease in his 60s and died over 10 years ago, but Mr. Volk witnessed his decline. He has noticed over the past year some mild memory issues but this is complicated by the fact that he was working as a personal financial adviser and his boss said he had slowed down and encouraged him to retire by 09/01/2012. Mr. Volk was getting very self conscious and was afraid of making mistakes. He retired by 05/02/2012. Since then he has noticed some more decline but his girlfriend Veksler feels he has some memory issues but they have not significantly increased or progressed. He is repeating some questions or forgetting recent conversations, but he keeps a calendar and has not missed any important meetings. During that time Veksler feels he was very stressed which made his memory worse. He pays his own bills but has not been using the excel spreadsheet to enter his finances, or do his budgeting, a task that he was previously quite fluent at both professionally and personally. He feels he has lost the drive and energy to do things. He is sometimes loosing track of what he wanted to say  and needs instructions to be repeated.    On a review of cognitive domains, he has noticed word finding problems but no other language or speech changes. He drives and has not gotten lost or had any recent accidents.     His motor function is fine. He walks daily one to two miles. He plays tennis and performs yoga, each about twice a week. He had no recent falls, no coordination or balance problems. He denies tremors in arms or hands.     Behaviorally, he denies any hallucinations or delusions. He sleeps well again but has some involuntary kicking at night, but no acting out or screaming. He and Veksler deny any behavioral or personality changes but both agree he seems to be more worried and anxious about his current problems. He feels he has less initiative and energy to do things and he is more relaying on Veksler to give him advice and directions on what to do. He continues to enjoy activities and has no vegetative sxs. He feels that his mood is ok.     Functionally, he is completely independent and lives alone but recently Veksler is more staying with him. He doesn't cook and eats out a lot usually fast food. He takes a vitamin in the morning but has no other prescription medication. Overall, he has not lost significant day to day function.     No past medical history on file.  No past surgical history on file.  No Known Allergies  Current Outpatient Prescriptions   Medication Sig Dispense Refill   ¿ MULTIVITAMIN W-MINERALS/LUTEIN (CENTRUM SILVER PO) take  by mouth.         ¿ SILDENAFIL CITRATE (VIAGRA PO) take  by mouth.             SOCIAL HISTORY: Mr. Volk has a BS in engineering from Princeton and a MBA from Stanford. He worked mainly as a financial adviser and planner for big companies. He retired in 06/01/2012 due to potential cognitive problems. He lives alone but Veksler stays over frequently. He drinks 1 to 2 beers every day but drunk more in the past. He stopped smoking 1978 after smoking 2 packs for about 8  years.     FAMILY HISTORY: Mother died at age 75 after a car accident.Father had a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease and Mr. Volk wasn't sure when it started, possibly in the mid 60s, but died in his 80's. He has 3 siblings. One sister age 62 is complaining of some memory issues, not being able to remember the tennis scores when she is playing. He has no children.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:  Complete review of systems is negative except for symptoms noted above and frequent urination and some numbness in feet.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  Vitals: Blood pressure 113/73, pulse 77, temperature 36.8 °C (98.2 °F), temperature source Oral, height 1.905 m (6' 3"), weight 79.35 kg (174 lb 15 oz).  General: Well appearing male in no distress. Normal, pleasant affect  HEENT: Normocephalic and atraumatic, eyes anicteric and non-injected, and oropharynx clear and moist.  No bruits. No C/C/E  No rashes    MSE: Alert, Ox3, nl speech and language. He looked to Veksler for details on history and needed clarification and sometimes repeating question because he forgot what I asked. Neuropsychological testing: The Mini Mental State Exam score was 29/30. He missed the day by one.  CN: 2-12 intact with PERRLA/EOMI/VFF; face, gag, SCM and tongue all sym.  Motor: nl tone, nl RMs, no drift, full strength, no tremor. No snout or grasp frontal release signs.  Coord: F/N and H/S nl  DTRs: 2+ bilat, plantars flexor bilat  Sensory: nl DSS, nl graphesthesia, mild loss in distal LE vibs beyond age, nl proprio  Gait: nl, 10/10 tandem, no retropulsion, neg Romberg.      PRIOR WORKUP: I thoroughly reviewed all medical records provided, including neuropsychological testing done by Dillion Swilley PhD in 08/01/2012. It showed some deficits in memory and language and mild deficits visual spatial reasoning. The results were not quantitative and the significance of the deficits were not clear. It was not clear if the deficits were quantitatively beyond age-associated  cognitive impairment which in this case is a key issue.       IMPRESSION: In sum, this is a 65 Y RH male with a concern for memory loss and perhaps apathy beyond that related to age. He does not appear to be depressed. His neuro exam today is normal. We will complete a full cognitive work up to look for the possibility of mild cognitive impairment (MCI) which could be a precursor for a more serious neurodegenerative disorder, esp given his family hx.     RECOMMENDATIONS:    (1) I will arrange for MRI brain here at Stanford, to screen for cerebrovascular disease or other structural brain abnormalities.    (2) I will arrange for a comprehensive neuropsychological evaluation here at Swanton , to determine the pattern and severity of any objective cognitive deficits.    (3) Regular, daily aerobic exercise can play a critical role in reducing the rate of cognitive and functional decline.    (4) Frequent socializing and other forms of mental stimulation can also contribute to a reduced rate of cognitive decline.    (5) I ordered TSH, free T4, and B12 as part of a routine screen for reversible causes of cognitive decline.     (6) Referral to a nutritionist to evaluate and educate Mr. Volk on healthy food and decreasing saturated fat intake.    Thank you again for the opportunity to participate in the care of Mr. Sophia Joffe Volk. I look forward to seeing him in two to three months after all the tests are done. In the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact me for questions or concerns at 999-999-9999.    Sincerely Yours,    Isami Swinney, MD, PhD  Professor of Neurology and Neurological Sciences  Stanford Center for Memory Disorders  999-999-9999

gold_text: Stanford Hospital and Clinics  Oncology New Patient Clinic Note    Today's Date:01/18/2015  Patient's Name: Walter Dawis   Medical Record Number: [000000]     Referring physician: Everette Akay, MD  498 N61st Dr, Glendale AZ     ID:  Mr. Woo is a 44 year-old Spanish-speaking gentleman with multiple recurrent, metastatic low-grade adenocarcinoma of the base of tongue s/p multiple resections and radiation treatments now with presumed recurrent disease involving the manubrium and a right axillary lymph node.      Oncologic History:     2001 - Patient was diagnosed with "tonsilar" mass biopsied as malignant, but we do not have those reports  2010 - Found to have low-grade adenocarcinoma of the oral cavity s/p excision  2011 - Hyoid recurrence s/p surgery and radiation treatment  2013 - Had contralateral neck recurrence s/p excision and XRT.           - March MRI with 1cm enhancing node in the R node of Woznicki, which resolved on a subsequent PET scan  without treatment           - Recurrence with right submandibular lymph node s/p excision. Pathology showing metastatic carcinoma  2014 - August PET scan showed hyperbolic focus in the right posterior manubrium           - November PET showed new focus in the superior manubrium, increasing FDG activity the posterior manubrium            lesion, and a new 7mm axillary lymph node, consistent with metastatic disease.     He is now here at the request of Dr. Nishimura for consideration of systemic treatment.     Interval History:  -- states that overall he feels well - feels like he doesn't have cancer  -- he does not endorse fatigue, adequate energy level  -- has occasional cramps in the back of his neck (4/10 pain)  -- stable appetite and weight, able to eat without dysphagia  -- no bony pain    A 14-point review of systems was otherwise negative.    Past Medical History:   None    Medications:  None    Family History:  Mother with cancer NOS    Social  History:   Lives in Island Park  currently. From Mexico originally, moved to US 2001. No family. He works 4-5 hours per day in a furniture store, also picks strawberries and blueberries.     Review Of Systems:   14 point ROS otherwise negative from above    Outpatient Medications:  None    No Known Allergies    Physical Exam  Vitals: BP 142/91 | Pulse 69 | Temp(Src) 36.7 °C (98.1 °F) (Oral) | Resp 17 | Ht 1.65 m (5' 4.96") | Wt 57.75 kg (127 lb 5.1 oz) | BMI 21.21 kg/m2 | SpO2 100%  ECOG performance status: 0  General: well appearing, no distress  Head/neck: thickened skin of the right neck with mild erythema, multiple well healed scars, subtle atrophy of the right shoulder. No masses visualized or palpated in the oropharynx. Radiation changes along the sternum.   Heart: rrr, no m/r/g  Lungs: ctab, no w/r/r  Abd: s/nt/nd +bs  Ext: no c/c/e  Skin: no rashes  Lymph: no palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Specifically, no axillary lymph nodes were palpable.  Neuro: grossly intact motor and sensory exam  Psych: normal affect and mentation      Labs:   None    Pathology:  10/05/2013:  DIAGNOSIS:  SPECIMEN SUBMITTED AS "SUBMANDIBULAR LYMPH NODE", RIGHT, excision  (10/05/2013; S13-9612)  -- METASTATIC POORLY DIFFERENTIATED CARCINOMA WITH FEATURES  SUGGESTIVE OF MUCOEPIDERMOID CARCINOMA    Imaging:  As above    Overall Impression  Mr. Mikkilineni is a 44 year-old Spanish-speaking gentleman with multiple recurrent, metastatic low-grade adenocarcinomas, originally of the base of tongue s/p multiple resections and radiation treatments now with likely recurrent disease involving the manubrium and a right axillary lymph node.      Overall, we are reassured that Mr. Platt is feeling quite well and is asymptomatic from his slow-growing disease. To further characterize his malignancy, we would like to obtain molecular profiling on his tissue to determine if he has a targetable mutation driving his cancer.     As  we have a tissue sample from his prior submandibular node biopsy, we will use that for molecular profiling.     We do not favor further treatment with surgery or radiation at this time, and given the slow-growing nature of his tumor biology, we would like to obtain molecular profiling information prior to proceeding with treatment.     We have asked him to return in 6 weeks once these studies are complete.     It was a pleasure meeting Mr. Dawis in our clinic today.     Plan:  --Send for molecular profiling on prior pathology  --f/u in 6 weeks to further discuss treatment options at that time  --will consider zometa treatment for bone metastases, but currently asymptomatic.     The patient was seen, examined, and discussed with the attending oncologist, Dr. Vijay, who agrees with the above plan of care.    01/18/2015 Doctor, MD    Bo Jahangiri, MD, MPH  Hematology/Oncology Fellow  Pager 14405  999-999-9999      I interviewed and examined the patient in clinic with Dr. Middleton.  I agree with the findings and recommendations documented in this note, which has been reviewed and edited by me to reflect my findings and contribution.

Return Patient Visit          Name: Sophia Driscoll  DOB: 06/20/1970  MRN: [000000]   Address:   15 Pgonzales Way, Chula Vista CA   Phone:    999-999-9999 (home)     Date: 05/01/2022   Attending Physician: Kersey Aguero Wallin, Centurion, Aarav Jones (Inactive)  370 Hcr 2124 Loop, Whitney TX     Referring Provider Unknown  No address on file    We had the pleasure of seeing your patient, Sophia Driscoll, in consultation today, 05/01/2022, at your request in the Department of Radiation Oncology at Stanford.    Identification: Mr. Koen Prout is a 51 Year-old male with multiple recurrent, metastatic low grade adenocarcinoma vs mucoepidermoid carcinoma of the base of tongue. He has undergone multiple resections and radiation treatments, most recently completed radiosurgery to two spine lesions at T3 (re-irradiation, 20 Gy in 1 fraction) and T10 (27 Gy in 3 fractions), completed on 06/25/2021. He recently underwent left frontal and occipital craniotomy at Bascom (Stigler ) for resection of tumor (path c/w metastatic carcinoma) followed by resection cavity SRS (27 Gy x 3 Fx, santa clara valley). His most recent thoracic spine imaging is significant for new metastatic disease at T1 vertebral body, now s/p SRS to this T1 lesion on 04/14/2022.      Interval Since Completion of Radiation Therapy:   6 years since completion of C2 SRS  2 years and 4 months since completion of C2/C3  2 years since completion of T10 SRS  1 year and 5 months since completion of T3 SRS  8 months since T3 (repeat) and T10  Brain cavity SRS at SCVMC  2 weeks since T1 SRS       Interval Events:   06/10/2021: Started Pembrolizumab    10/24/2021: Admitted to the hospital with AMS. Discharged home on 11/01/2021  10/25/2021: MR Brain  IMPRESSION:   1.  Re demonstration of left frontal and left parietal lobe cystic hemorrhagic metastases with peripheral enhancement and surrounding vasogenic edema. There is associated left to right midline  shift measuring 1.1 cm with severe mass effect on the left lateral ventricle with contralateral trapping of the right temporal horn and trans ependymal CSF flow indicating acute hydrocephalus.  There is additionally effacement of the left ambient cistern with mass effect on the midbrain consistent with descending transtentorial and uncal herniation.   2.  Re demonstration of soft tissue lesions in the posterior scalp concerning for metastases.   3.  T1 hypointense lesions in C2 and C3 concerning for bony metastases.       10/29/2021: Underwent L frontal and L parieto-occipital craniotomies for resection of brain metastases.   Pathology:   A: Brain, Left Frontal Tumor, Resection: Metastatic carcinoma.  B: Brain, Tissue Aspirate// Left Occiput, Resection: Metastatic carcinoma. gold_text: IMPRESSION:   MRI BRAIN: No acute intracranial hemorrhage, infarct or hydrocephalus.  Probable neurofibroma along the left frontal to left periorbital region. Left side phthisis bulbi. MRI CERVICAL SPINE: 1. No high-grade stenosis, neuroforaminal narrowing or cord abnormalities. 2.  Probable neurofibromas within the right scalene muscles and in the region of the right brachial plexus. I, Rebekah Caputo M.D. have reviewed this radiologic study personally and I am in full agreement with the findings of the report presented.  MRI BRAIN WITH CONTRAST MRI CERVICAL SPINE WITHOUT CONTRAST HISTORY:  neurofibromatosis COMPARISON: None available. TECHNIQUE: MRI of the brain was performed on a 1.5 T imaging system using sagittal T1W, axial T1W, DWI, T2W FSE, FLAIR, and postcontrast axial and coronal T1W images. MRI of the cervical spine was performed using sagittal T1, T2, STIR, axial T1 and T2  sequences. A total of 12 mL of Magnevist was injected intravenously without complications. FINDINGS:  MRI BRAIN: Examination is degraded by patient motion. There is extensive swelling of the left frontal to left periorbital region, which demonstrates mild enhancement, likely representing a neurofibroma. Small amount of extraconal orbital fat is seen herniating along the inferolateral aspect of the orbit,  which may be secondary to sphenoid wing dysplasia. No acute hemorrhage, infarct, hydrocephalus or midline shift is seen. The ventricles are normal sized.  There is an anterior-posterior orientation abnormal cleft in the right cerebellum; suggesting developmental anomaly. The basal cisterns are patent.  There is no extra-axial fluid collection. The post contrast images do not show any areas of abnormal enhancement of the brain parenchyma, leptomeninges or ependyma.   The major intracranial blood vessels demonstrate normal flow voids consistent with their patency.  The left globe is deformed and there is a probable prosthesis anterior to the deformed globe.  Mild mucosal thickening is seen in the left maxillary sinus. MRI CERVICAL SPINE: There is straightening of cervical lordosis. The vertebral body heights, marrow signal, alignment and disc heights are maintained. There is no significant spinal canal stenosis or neural foraminal narrowing at any level. The thoracic spinal cord is  normal in signal characteristics and caliber. There are multilobulated T2 hyperintense masses within the right scalene muscles and in the region of the right brachial plexus, likely representing neurofibromas.

Referral:  Farr, Carl Frederickson, MD  6560 Espinoza 663 Ysleta Ln, El Paso TX       Vestibular Evaluation  Videonystagmography (VNG)  Vestibular Evoked Myogenic Potentials (VEMP)  Video Head Impulse Test (VHIT)      History:    Mrs. Li Gladis Partin is a 69-year-old female who presents in Audiology today for vestibular assessment. She was accompanied by her husband for support. Patient reports life-long history of motion sensitivity. She reports intermittent dizziness since 2017, now with constant dizziness and new onset of imbalance over the past several years. Symptoms are getting worse. Any type of movement exacerbates dizziness (e.g. bending down, looking to the right/left/up). She also reports a history of headaches.     The Dizziness Handicap Inventory (DHI) was developed to evaluate the self-perceived handicapping effects imposed by the vestibular system disease. (Jacobsen & Newman 1995)     DHI total score: 88 (out of 100) which is considered a severe handicap.     Sub-scales--  Emotional:  32% Functional:  36% Physical:  32%    Each external auditory canal was found to be free from occlusion.     Interpretation:    VNG testing revealed robust and symmetrical caloric responses, with no evidence of peripheral vestibulopathy. Fixation suppression was appropriate. Dix-Hallpike testing was negative for BPPV, though some low grade, persistent (non-fatigable) left-beating nystagmus was observed during head-hanging left.   Occulomotor testing was within normal limits for saccades, smooth pursuit, gaze, and OPK.     The results of cVEMP testing indicated present responses bilaterally. This finding suggests normal saccule, inferior vestibular nerve, and descending vestibulo-colic reflex pathway function.    The results of 500 Hz air conduction oVEMP testing indicated present response for the right ear and absent response for the left ear, indicating dysfunction of the utricle on the left side. However,  bone conduction oVEMP testing showed small,but present responses bilaterally, suggesting some residual utricular function.    While this may be associated with peripheral dysfunction, abnormalities in oVEMP testing have been noted in patients with headache and motion intolerance. Of note, patients older than 60-years-old are less likely to demonstrate reliable responses.     The results of 4000 Hz air conduction oVEMP testing indicated absent responses bilaterally, suggesting absence of a third-window phenomenon. This is a normal finding.    Video Head Impulse Testing (vHIT) was normal for high-velocity head rotations stimulating all six semi-circular canals. Results suggest an ability to maintain normal fixation through the vestibulo-ocular reflex pathway.      Recommendation:    1) Follow up with Dr. Alfredo Tannous, M.D. as scheduled.    Shawn Wilkening, Au.D. CCC-A  Clinical Audiologist      TEST  RESULTS:    VNG Testing    Spontaneous Gaze: 2°/sec left-beating nystagmus present; suppressed with fixation.            Gaze-Evoked:  No nystagmus observed in any condition (leftward, rightward, upward, downward).         Random Saccade:  Normal gain, latency, and accuracy.    Smooth Pursuit:  Normal gain with smooth eye movement.        Optokinetics (OPK): Normal eye movement.  Not tested in full-field.      Head Shake:  No nystagmus observed following 15-second head shake.      Positioning:   Dix-Hallpike testing revealed low-grade persistent, non-fatigable left beating nystagmus during head-hanging left. Patient also reported sensations of movement.          Static Positional:  Non-clinically significant nystagmus was observed as follows with vision denied:      Head Right:  1°/sec left-beating nystagmus      Head Left:  2°/sec left-beating nystagmus     In all positions, nystagmus suppressed with fixation.        Caloric Testing: Binaural monothermal caloric testing was completed using air irrigation and yielded  the following results:      Right Warm: 44°/sec right-beating      Left Warm: 36°/sec left-beating      Asymmetry:  10% right stronger      Total Response: 80°/sec      Patient was able to fixate to a target light during testing.          Video Head Impulse Testing:     Procedure Description: The Video Head Impulse Test (vHIT) assesses the function of the peripheral balance organs located in the inner ear known as semicircular canals. The test utilizes special goggles equipped with an eye-tacking camera and head-tracking motion sensor. These goggles are worn by the patient as they fixate on a stationary visual target while the examiner applies several brief, quick, and small impulses to the head in 3 planes of movement that correspond to the orientation of the semicircular canals. The head and eye movements are recorded and analyzed. The average time to complete and analyze this test, including all 3 planes, is about 40 minutes. This information enables healthcare providers to make appropriate treatment recommendations and is integral to an objective vestibular evaluation. The patient tolerated the testing well.
