question: This is a complex whole body PET/MR report. Provide a version of this report that is more concise and easier to read for the referring physician.
evidence: procedure note" start="01/33/2018 03:02 PM">PET MRI
gold_text: US GUIDED INGUINAL LYMPH NODE BIOPSY RIGHT  09/22/2017 PROVIDED CLINICAL INDICATIONS:  R59.0: Localized enlarged lymph nodes. ADDITIONAL CLINICAL HISTORY:  None. COMPARISON:  None. TECHNIQUE:  Following a PARQ conference, written informed consent was obtained from the patient.  Prior to beginning the procedure, a standard team pause was performed. Moderate sedation achieved with intravenous fentanyl and Versed during continuous monitoring of vital signs by a trained nurse.  Total duration of sedation was approximately 30 minutes. Following a PARQ conference, written informed consent was obtained from the patient.  Prior to beginning the procedure, a standard team pause was performed. Surgical safety checklist performed. The patient was placed supine on the procedure table.  Sterile preparation of the right groin was performed; maximal sterile barrier technique followed including: cap and mask and sterile gown and sterile gloves and a large sterile sheet and hand hygiene and 2% chlorhexidine for cutaneous antisepsis. Sterile gel and sterile probe covers were utilized for ultrasound guidance.  Local anesthesia achieved with 1% lidocaine solution subcutaneously. Under CT  guidance, a 19-gauge introducer needle was advanced into the lesion.  The inner stylet was removed and a 20-gauge core biopsy device advanced.  8 core samples were collected.  Samples were submitted for surgical pathology and flow cytometry.   The introducer needle was removed and access site dressed with a sterile bandage.  Completion ultrasoundobtained.  The patient tolerated the procedure well without immediate complication. FINDINGS:   Initial ultrasound redemonstrates the right groin lesion.    Intraprocedural images document the access needle projecting to the lesion.     Completion images show no evidence of acute complication. IMPRESSION- Successful ultrasound-guided biopsy of dominant right groin lymph node with a total of 8 20-gauge core samples collected and submitted for surgical pathology and flow cytometry.     Dictated by: Taganas M.D. on 09/22/2017  Electronically signed by: Pla M.D. on 09/22/2017 PSR_OR

CLINICAL HISTORY:  50 year old female with the following ICD-9 code designations: 088.81, 627.2 and 279.4.  No prior hematologic studies are on record in the pathology database.

PERIPHERAL BLOOD: The red cells are normal in number and are normochromic and normocytic with minimal variation in size and shape.  Leukocytes are normal in number overall, consisting of similar numbers of segmented neutrophils and lymphocytes, with fewer monocytes.  Some lymphocytes with reactive morphology and large granular lymphocytes are present, though neither are significantly increased.  No circulating plasma cells are seen, nor is there any rouleaux formation.  No circulating blast cells or dysplastic changes are identified.  Platelets are normal in number and normal in morphology.

FLOW CYTOMETRIC IMMUNOPHENOTYPING: LYMPHOMA PANEL A
Specimen type:  PB

CD16+/CD3- (NK subset)             6
CD56+/CD3+ (T subset)              13
CD56+/CD3- (NK)                         6
CD57+/CD3+ (T subset)    11
CD57+/CD3- (NK subset)   4

INTERPRETATION:
Flow cytometry was performed on the peripheral blood primarily to evaluate lymphocytes.  The lymphocyte gate contains a heterogeneous population of lymphocytes, mostly T cells with fewer B- and NK cells.  B cells show polytypic light chain expression and unremarkable B cell antigen expression.  T cells consist predominantly of CD4 T-cells, with relatively reduced numbers of CD8 T-cells.  Overall, T-cells show unremarkable T cell antigen expression, without evidence of aberrant antigen coexpression or loss.  NK cells show expected reactivity with panel antibodies.  There is no significant increase in the proportion of T cells expressing NK-related antigens CD16, CD56 or CD57.

A gate on monocytes and other large mononuclear cells (numeric data not reported) contains predominantly CD4+, CD3-negative cells most consistent with monocytes.
This immunologic test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by Stanford University Flow Cytometry Laboratory .  Unless indicated otherwise, it has not been cleared or approved by the Sparks , although such approval is not required for analyte-specific reagents of this type. Flow cytometry testing performed at 987 Koopman Dr, Cambria WI .    Yechiel Gregory, M.D. - Laboratory Director






COMMENT:  There are no morphologic or flow immunophenotypic findings to suggest involvement by a B-cell lymphoma or abnormal T-cell population.  Lymphocytes are not increased and show a mild CD4+ subset predominance.  Clinical correlation is recommended.

DIAGNOSIS:
PERIPHERAL BLOOD
--   NO DIAGNOSTIC ABNORMALITY

FLOW CYTOMETRIC IMMUNOPHENOTYPING:
--   MIXED LYMPHOCYTES WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF B-CELL MONOCLONALITY OR ABERRANT T-CELL MARKER EXPRESSION (SEE COMMENT)

Patient's daughter Teagle would is her medical decision maker agrees and plans to coordinate outpatient EGD. Strict return precautions discussed, with patient's daughter who verbalizes understanding.

gold_text: Stanford Center for Movement Disorders    12/25/2013    Sunio, Sol Hedin, MD  Visalia Medical Clinic  947 X48 Rd, Placerville CO     RE:    Norris, Burnell#   [000000]  DOB  02/25/1944    Dear Dr.  Sunio.,    We have the pleasure of seeing Mr. Krysta Norris, a 69 Y R-handed male in consultation at the Stanford Center for Memory Disorders for possible Alzheimer's Disease.     Mr. Norris presents today with his wife for further evaluation. Mr. Norris's wife, Kump, describes a 10-year slowly progressive memory decline. The patient notes his memory decline has been noticeable over the past 1-2 years. His memory difficulties are characterized by short-term memory deficits. He at times does not recall individuals' names or where he has left items. He also has difficulties recalling a list of tasks he should complete. He does not experience difficulties recalling where he parked his car or with spatial orientation. He notes no difficulties with parking. His mother reports that the patient repeats the same stories. His wife does not recall episodes of Mr. Norris repeating stories or asking questions repeatedly. He was started on donepezil and namenda (unclear sequence) over the past 1.5 years and notes that it halted his memory decline.    Of note, he has a longstanding history of forgetting appointments dating back to his young adulthood.    As regards other nonmemory symptoms:  He denies any significant word-finding trouble. He notes a postural tremor in his R hand that began 30 years prior. The tremor worsens with certain activities such as writing, drinking. He also has a family history of tremor. With bending forward to pick up a golf ball he notes feeling lightheaded though denies a similar experience when getting up from bed quickly. He and his wife deny falls, bradykinesia.    His wife and the patient note greater irritability typically related to difficulties recalling. His  threshold for frustration is lowering with responses increasing. The patient and his wife specifically deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm during frustrated episodes.     His wife notes excessive snoring in the evenings as well as jerking movements which shake the bed over the past 5 years. The movements can occur in all extremities and are characterized by a series of jerking movement that cease after a few seconds. The movements do not occur during the day. He notes multiple episodes of his jaws clamping down with associated tongue biting during his sleep, 2-3x/year. His wife denies that the patient acts out his dreams. The patient denies vivid dreams.     He reports occasional constipation that is not particularly problematic. He denies changes in his urinary habits. He reports erectile dysfunction beginning in his late 40s. Of note, he receives testosterone shots monthly for low testosterone.    As regards seizure risk factors, he denies a history of head trauma, meningitis/encephalitis, episodes of LOC, stroke, seizures.    He denies difficulties with activities of daily living including bathing, dressing, feeding himself. He manages his own medications. His wife pays the bills and always had, this is not a change.     PMH:  HLD  Skin cancer  Spasmodic dysphonia first discovered in 90s for which he receives botox injections  Back pain    PSH:  Rectal surgery    MEDS:  Testosterone  B12 1000mcg PO DAILY  Donepezil 10mg PO every other day  namenda 10mg PO BID  dexilant 60mg PO DAILY  Simvastatin 20mg PO QHS  Cyclobenzaprine 10mg PO BID  Tramadol ER 200mg PO DAILY  botox injections for spasmodic dysphonia  Hydrocodone/APAP 5/325 PO BID PRN  claritin  colace    Allergy: Review of patient's allergies indicates no known allergies.    SH: He previously worked for the Sheriff's Department and currently works in the local community college. He completed 2 years of college. He lives with his wife in  Garrison . He previously smoked for many years and quit several years ago. He chewed tobacco for 8 years which he quit several years ago. He drinks a glass of wine every evening.    FH: Father with colon cancer. No family history of dementia. Family members with tremor    Review of Systems: Pertinent positives are documented above. A 14-system review was performed and was otherwise unremarkable.       Review of previous medical records:    Physical Examination: BP 120/74 | Pulse 80 | Ht 1.829 m (6') | Wt 99.791 kg (220 lb) | BMI 29.83 kg/m2.  Lying BP 130/79, HR 60  Standing 122/70, HR 72  He appears well and comfortable. Head is normocephalic and atraumatic, oral pharynx is clear without lesions, mucous membranes moist, no cervical lymphadenopathy, neck is supple without rigidity, limbs are warm, well perfused, without obvious skin lesions and without edema.     MS: He is attentive, oriented, and has normal memory and fund of knowledge. Speech is raspy though comprehensible. Language is fluent in expression with intact ability to follow complex commands and answer questions appropriately.   MOCA was completed, with a score of 26/30 with points deducted for word recall.   CN: PERRL, EOMI, face symmetric in strength and sensation, palate raises symmetrically, tongue midline, SCM and trapezius full in strength  Motor: Normal bulk. Postural tremor of RUE>LUE. No resting tremor. Mildly increased tone with activation though no baseline rigidity. No cogwheeling. Intact and symmetric finger / foot taps. Full strength throughout.  Sensation: Intact to light touch throughout  Coordination: FNF intact, HKS intact  Gait: Fluid and normal with intact heel and toe walking, and intact and normal tandem gait. There is no Romberg sign.      Data: Head CT from 12/12 reviewed. No significant atrophy or infarcts.    Impression:  Mr. Shank is a 69 y/o M with a history of HLD presenting for further evaluation of memory deficits  beginning 10 years prior though most prominent over the past 1 to 2 years. His memory deficits are characterized by short-term recall deficits. These deficits are accompanied by frustration. His physical exam is notable for a raspy voice and postural tremor. Work-up thus far includes normal B12 and head CT that is unremarkable. His symptoms are consistent with mild cognitive impairment. He does not exhibit any prominent signs of Parkinsonism (he may have essential tremor) or report symptoms concerning for Lewy Body Dementia. To further assess his mild cognitive impairment we recommend evaluation with an MRI Brain, neuropsychological testing, and checking TSH, RPR. As regards medication recommendations, we recommend minimizing the use of sedating medications such as cyclobenzaprine, tramadol, and vicodin. We would also recommend discontinuing namenda as this can cause confusion and is indicated for moderate-severe dementia. It is possible that a good component of his cognitive trouble is medication related.    As regards his wife's complaints of sleep disturbance, this is characterized by jerking movements during sleep. The semiology of the events is not suggestive of REM behavior disorder and more likely myoclonic jerking during sleep. However, given that we do not have a clear description of the events we would like to obtain a spot EEG to assess for ictal/interictal activity.    The patient has a postural tremor, likely an essential tremor given his family history and the quality of the tremor. The patient currently denies any disturbance as a result of this tremor, therefore, we would not recommend any medication management at this time.     The patient's frustrated episodes in relation to his memory deficits were discussed at length. His wife reports some concern that these may worsen though the patient and his wife deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm. The patient does have guns at home which his  wife assures us will be removed from the home when she insists upon it. We have counseled the patient's wife to call 911 should she be concerned about the possibility of physical harm. She understand this plan. The patient additionally understands that he should monitor his frustration.    Recommendations:  -We would recommend checking TSH, RPR to check reversible causes of cognitive impairement  -We will obtain an MRI 3T Memory Protocol  -We will check a spot EEG to evaluate for ictal/interictal activity given jerking movements during sleep with tongue biting  -We will order Neuropsychological testing to further evaluate cognitive impairment  -As regards medication adjustments, we have recommended tapering off of namenda. In addition, we have recommended minimizing the use of sedating medications including cyclobenzaprine, vicodin, and tramadol as these may further impair memory.  -Continue donepezil at this time  -RTC in 7 months    Thank you for the referral.    Sincerely,    Maris, MD   1:28 PM, 12/25/2013    I examined and discussed the patient with Dr. Centanni.  I have edited her excellent note and now agree with it in its entirety.  Townsend, MD  Assistant Professor

Few scattered melanocytic nevi   Clinical features of atypical nevi discussed  -  Patient  to continue monitoring clinically and self skin examination,    - Sun protection discussed and advised   - Patient advised to return to clinic if notices any new or changing lesions.      Return to Clinic: PRN     Luria Siegel, MD

gold_text: Stanford Center for Movement Disorders    12/25/2013    Sunio, Sol Hedin, MD  Visalia Medical Clinic  947 X48 Rd, Placerville CO     RE:    Norris, Burnell#   [000000]  DOB  02/25/1944    Dear Dr.  Sunio.,    We have the pleasure of seeing Mr. Krysta Norris, a 69 Y R-handed male in consultation at the Stanford Center for Memory Disorders for possible Alzheimer's Disease.     Mr. Norris presents today with his wife for further evaluation. Mr. Norris's wife, Kump, describes a 10-year slowly progressive memory decline. The patient notes his memory decline has been noticeable over the past 1-2 years. His memory difficulties are characterized by short-term memory deficits. He at times does not recall individuals' names or where he has left items. He also has difficulties recalling a list of tasks he should complete. He does not experience difficulties recalling where he parked his car or with spatial orientation. He notes no difficulties with parking. His mother reports that the patient repeats the same stories. His wife does not recall episodes of Mr. Norris repeating stories or asking questions repeatedly. He was started on donepezil and namenda (unclear sequence) over the past 1.5 years and notes that it halted his memory decline.    Of note, he has a longstanding history of forgetting appointments dating back to his young adulthood.    As regards other nonmemory symptoms:  He denies any significant word-finding trouble. He notes a postural tremor in his R hand that began 30 years prior. The tremor worsens with certain activities such as writing, drinking. He also has a family history of tremor. With bending forward to pick up a golf ball he notes feeling lightheaded though denies a similar experience when getting up from bed quickly. He and his wife deny falls, bradykinesia.    His wife and the patient note greater irritability typically related to difficulties recalling. His  threshold for frustration is lowering with responses increasing. The patient and his wife specifically deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm during frustrated episodes.     His wife notes excessive snoring in the evenings as well as jerking movements which shake the bed over the past 5 years. The movements can occur in all extremities and are characterized by a series of jerking movement that cease after a few seconds. The movements do not occur during the day. He notes multiple episodes of his jaws clamping down with associated tongue biting during his sleep, 2-3x/year. His wife denies that the patient acts out his dreams. The patient denies vivid dreams.     He reports occasional constipation that is not particularly problematic. He denies changes in his urinary habits. He reports erectile dysfunction beginning in his late 40s. Of note, he receives testosterone shots monthly for low testosterone.    As regards seizure risk factors, he denies a history of head trauma, meningitis/encephalitis, episodes of LOC, stroke, seizures.    He denies difficulties with activities of daily living including bathing, dressing, feeding himself. He manages his own medications. His wife pays the bills and always had, this is not a change.     PMH:  HLD  Skin cancer  Spasmodic dysphonia first discovered in 90s for which he receives botox injections  Back pain    PSH:  Rectal surgery    MEDS:  Testosterone  B12 1000mcg PO DAILY  Donepezil 10mg PO every other day  namenda 10mg PO BID  dexilant 60mg PO DAILY  Simvastatin 20mg PO QHS  Cyclobenzaprine 10mg PO BID  Tramadol ER 200mg PO DAILY  botox injections for spasmodic dysphonia  Hydrocodone/APAP 5/325 PO BID PRN  claritin  colace    Allergy: Review of patient's allergies indicates no known allergies.    SH: He previously worked for the Sheriff's Department and currently works in the local community college. He completed 2 years of college. He lives with his wife in  Garrison . He previously smoked for many years and quit several years ago. He chewed tobacco for 8 years which he quit several years ago. He drinks a glass of wine every evening.    FH: Father with colon cancer. No family history of dementia. Family members with tremor    Review of Systems: Pertinent positives are documented above. A 14-system review was performed and was otherwise unremarkable.       Review of previous medical records:    Physical Examination: BP 120/74 | Pulse 80 | Ht 1.829 m (6') | Wt 99.791 kg (220 lb) | BMI 29.83 kg/m2.  Lying BP 130/79, HR 60  Standing 122/70, HR 72  He appears well and comfortable. Head is normocephalic and atraumatic, oral pharynx is clear without lesions, mucous membranes moist, no cervical lymphadenopathy, neck is supple without rigidity, limbs are warm, well perfused, without obvious skin lesions and without edema.     MS: He is attentive, oriented, and has normal memory and fund of knowledge. Speech is raspy though comprehensible. Language is fluent in expression with intact ability to follow complex commands and answer questions appropriately.   MOCA was completed, with a score of 26/30 with points deducted for word recall.   CN: PERRL, EOMI, face symmetric in strength and sensation, palate raises symmetrically, tongue midline, SCM and trapezius full in strength  Motor: Normal bulk. Postural tremor of RUE>LUE. No resting tremor. Mildly increased tone with activation though no baseline rigidity. No cogwheeling. Intact and symmetric finger / foot taps. Full strength throughout.  Sensation: Intact to light touch throughout  Coordination: FNF intact, HKS intact  Gait: Fluid and normal with intact heel and toe walking, and intact and normal tandem gait. There is no Romberg sign.      Data: Head CT from 12/12 reviewed. No significant atrophy or infarcts.    Impression:  Mr. Shank is a 69 y/o M with a history of HLD presenting for further evaluation of memory deficits  beginning 10 years prior though most prominent over the past 1 to 2 years. His memory deficits are characterized by short-term recall deficits. These deficits are accompanied by frustration. His physical exam is notable for a raspy voice and postural tremor. Work-up thus far includes normal B12 and head CT that is unremarkable. His symptoms are consistent with mild cognitive impairment. He does not exhibit any prominent signs of Parkinsonism (he may have essential tremor) or report symptoms concerning for Lewy Body Dementia. To further assess his mild cognitive impairment we recommend evaluation with an MRI Brain, neuropsychological testing, and checking TSH, RPR. As regards medication recommendations, we recommend minimizing the use of sedating medications such as cyclobenzaprine, tramadol, and vicodin. We would also recommend discontinuing namenda as this can cause confusion and is indicated for moderate-severe dementia. It is possible that a good component of his cognitive trouble is medication related.    As regards his wife's complaints of sleep disturbance, this is characterized by jerking movements during sleep. The semiology of the events is not suggestive of REM behavior disorder and more likely myoclonic jerking during sleep. However, given that we do not have a clear description of the events we would like to obtain a spot EEG to assess for ictal/interictal activity.    The patient has a postural tremor, likely an essential tremor given his family history and the quality of the tremor. The patient currently denies any disturbance as a result of this tremor, therefore, we would not recommend any medication management at this time.     The patient's frustrated episodes in relation to his memory deficits were discussed at length. His wife reports some concern that these may worsen though the patient and his wife deny any physical harm or intent to physically harm. The patient does have guns at home which his  wife assures us will be removed from the home when she insists upon it. We have counseled the patient's wife to call 911 should she be concerned about the possibility of physical harm. She understand this plan. The patient additionally understands that he should monitor his frustration.    Recommendations:  -We would recommend checking TSH, RPR to check reversible causes of cognitive impairement  -We will obtain an MRI 3T Memory Protocol  -We will check a spot EEG to evaluate for ictal/interictal activity given jerking movements during sleep with tongue biting  -We will order Neuropsychological testing to further evaluate cognitive impairment  -As regards medication adjustments, we have recommended tapering off of namenda. In addition, we have recommended minimizing the use of sedating medications including cyclobenzaprine, vicodin, and tramadol as these may further impair memory.  -Continue donepezil at this time  -RTC in 7 months    Thank you for the referral.    Sincerely,    Maris, MD   1:28 PM, 12/25/2013    I examined and discussed the patient with Dr. Centanni.  I have edited her excellent note and now agree with it in its entirety.  Townsend, MD  Assistant Professor

question: Could this patient's symptoms be attributed to a side effect of one of their current medications?
evidence: This raises concern in light of known kidney stone side effect secondary to Topamax use.
gold_text: NEUROLOGY CONSULTATION    ***ALLERGIES***The patient has no known drug allergies.    CHIEF COMPLAINT: Slurred speech, word-finding difficulty, headache.    HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Mr. Amos is a 31-year-old white  gentleman with a history of depression and hypertension who presented to  the emergency room on the evening of 03/01/2024 complaining of left  retro-orbital pain associated with slurred speech and inappropriate words.  The patient stated that his symptoms began around 10 a.m., shortly after  awakening, with problems with slurred speech and inappropriate words. The  patient said he had some numbness and tingling in his left hand and, most  remarkably, the tips of his fingers. The patient denied any pain or other  numbness at that time. The patient states that he has some baseline  left-sided weakness, which is mild, but that his weakness did not become  acutely worse during this episode. The patient said that symptoms  continued until he developed acute onset of left-sided retro-orbital pain  that began around 6 p.m. the evening of admission. The pain was rated at  approximately 7/10. The patient denied any other symptoms, such as  palpitations, shortness of breath, or nausea. The patient stated that he  had some blurry vision, but no flashing lights; the patient denied any  diplopia. The patient said that he also developed some fatigue at this  time.    The patient states that this type of thing has happened to him before. He  has had three episodes, the first of which was in 10/27/2001. The  patient states that he began noticing that he felt a little bit lightheaded  and had some problems with balance. The patient stated that an hour later,  he noticed that his left hand was remarkably weak and numb. The patient  stated that he had some numbness of his lips and blurry vision. The  patient also developed fairly acute onset left-sided retro-orbital pain  that he rated at approximately  9/10. The patient also recalled having  problems with word finding and speaking inappropriate words at that time.  He stated that he also had two additional episodes between 10/27/2001 and  the current presentation. During those episodes, the patient stated that  his symptoms and his headaches were significantly less severe. The patient  rated those headaches at approximately 4/10 to 5/10. In all of these  episodes, the patient denied any palpitations, shortness of breath, nausea,  or diplopia. The patient states that he had blurry vision and some fatigue  associated with these episodes.    During his first episode, the patient presented to an emergency room in  Delta , where he was told that he had a complicated migraine. The patient  was discharged from the ER with a referral to Dr. Zechariah Zambito, a  neurologist. The patient saw Dr. Padgett after his first episode, and he  had an MRI. Based on that MRI, the patient states that Dr. Ghosh told  him that he may have had a mild stroke. The patient presents to the  emergency room today because he was concerned that he may have had another  stroke.    PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Depression, hypertension over the past five years;  the patient says that he has hypertensive episodes with blood pressure  going up to 160/120 over a baseline blood pressure of 120/70 on medication.  The patient states that he feels "strange" when he has these hypertensive  episodes.    MEDICATIONS  1. Diovan 160 mg q.d.  2. Propanolol 20 mg b.i.d.  3. Effexor XR 150 mg q.d.    SOCIAL HISTORY: The patient is an undergraduate student studying English at  Alonie College. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. The patient lives at home  with his partner.    FAMILY HISTORY: The patient does not know his father. The patient states  that his mother is 69 years old and has hypertension, a thyroid condition,  and some heart problems. The patient has a brother who had a seizure as a  young child, but does not  have epilepsy.    REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: The patient denied any chest pain, shortness of breath,  nausea or vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, or hematuria. The  patient denies any heat or cold intolerance. The patient denies any head  trauma, any history of intracranial infections, or any family history of  seizures.    PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: VITAL SIGNS: Stable and unremarkable. NEUROLOGICAL:  The patient was alert and oriented x 3. He was grossly cognitively intact.  The patient had fluid speech, with normal naming and repetition. There was  no obvious aphasia or dysarthria at the time of the exam. His pupils were  equally round and reactive to light. Extraocular movements were intact.  There was no nystagmus. His tongue and palate were midline. Facial  expression was symmetric. Facial sensation was intact and symmetric as  well. The patient's hearing was intact. The patient's sternocleidomastoid  and trapezius were 5/5 in strength. The patient's motor strength was 5/5  in all extremities. He had normal tone and bulk. There was no pronator  drift. The patient's finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin were within normal  limits. His sensation was intact to light touch and symmetric. His gait  was unremarkable. The patient had a normal Romberg. His reflexes were 2+  throughout, and his toes were downgoing bilaterally.    LABORATORY STUDIES: The patient had an unremarkable chem-7. He had a CBC  that was only remarkable for mildly elevated white count at 12.6.    IMAGING STUDIES: The patient's head CT demonstrated no acute disease.  There was no bleed and no evidence of an old infarct. Review of the  patient's MRI, which the patient brought with him to the emergency room,  demonstrated a 1 x 4-mm area that was bright on FLAIR. This abnormality  was seen only on one slice and was located superior to the left basal  ganglia. This abnormality was also subtly appreciated on T2 imaging. This  abnormality was not seen on T1  imaging and did not enhance on the post  Gadolinium images.    IMPRESSION: The patient is a 31-year-old gentleman with depression and  hypertension who presents to us with slurred speech, inappropriate words,  associated with left-sided numbness and tingling and left-sided  retro-orbital pain. These episodes have occurred numerous times in the  past beginning in 10/27/2001. The patient's clinical picture and  presentation are most consistent with a complicated migraine. The patient  may, in fact, be partially treated with his current antihypertensive  medication, propanolol. The patient has been seeing Dr. Harold Foley;  however, the patient would like a referral to one of our headache  specialists here at Stanford. We will arrange for the patient to follow up  with Dr. Tyson Yaghoubian in our neurology clinic at the earliest possible  date. This plan was discussed in detail with the patient, and the patient  demonstrated complete understanding.    RECOMMENDATIONS  1. Discharge the patient to home from the emergency room.  2. The patient is to follow up with Dr. Graysen Swain. The patient will   be notified by the neurology clinic for an appointment.    Grewe, Aguilar, M.D.,Ph.D.    cc: Mcewen, M.D.   Room H-3160/ MC 5235, RTE 2     Baldomero Clough, M.D., Ph.D.   Room A343  d: 03/03/2002  t: 03/04/2002/mmt  i: 03/06/2002/mmt/af    doc: [000000]  15-1101

gold_text: IMPRESSION: Benign findings. BI-RADS 2. Recommend routine mammographic followup.     In accordance with state law, the following is included in the patient's notification letter: "Your mammogram shows that your breast tissue is dense. Dense breast tissue is common and is not abnormal. However, dense breast tissue can make it harder to evaluate the results of your mammogram and may also be associated with an increased risk of breast cancer. This information about the results of your mammogram is given to you to raise your awareness and to inform your conversations with your doctor. Together, you can decide which screening options are right for you. A report of your results was sent to your physician." Dictated and signed by: Thorin Kukla, MD 12/24/2016 REASON FOR EXAM: Screening TECHNIQUE: Full field bilateral digital mammography was performed.  The images are also evaluated with R2 computer-aided detection software.  COMPARISON: None available The fibroglandular tissue is extremely dense which may obscure lesions and diminishes the sensitivity of screening mammography for breast cancer. There are no masses, suspicious microcalcifications or secondary signs of malignancy.        Benign calcifications are seen bilaterally. BREAST CANCER RISK: Mensch model lifetime risk of invasive breast cancer is 9.6%, estimation by patient provided Abell.
